Ayah 1–16 · linked video: 01. Fussilat (Ayah 1-16)

Surah Fussilat: Ayahs 1-16

Ayah 1

حمٓ

This Surah is also known as Ha Meem As-Sajdah to distinguish it from Surah As-Sajdah (Surah 32), as both contain a verse requiring prostration. The primary name, however, is Fussilat, which we’ll explore in the coming ayahs.

Ayah 2

تَنزِيلٌ مِّنَ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنِ ٱلرَّحِيمِ

A great revelation from Ar-Rahman, Ar-Raheem—the Exceedingly Merciful, the Ultimately Merciful, the Always Merciful. The word تَنزِيلٌ carries the meaning of magnificence and elevation. It’s not simply something that came down, but something magnificent that descended from the highest realm. This opening immediately establishes the divine source and nature of what follows.

Ayah 3

كِتَـٰبٌ فُصِّلَتْ ءَايَـٰتُهُۥ قُرْءَانًا عَرَبِيًّا لِّقَوْمٍ يَعْلَمُونَ

An incredible Book whose ayahs—whose smallest units, whose miracles, whose declarations—have been clarified and explained in detail. The word فُصِّلَتْ comes from the root that means to separate things from one another and to ensure that every part is understood distinctly. When someone speaks too fast and you can’t follow, you say, “Slow down, break it up piece by piece.” When dealing with a complicated problem, the way to make it digestible is to take one thing, separate it from the next, then the next, so you understand how everything fits together.

This is the methodology of explanation that fussilat provides. While tafseer means to explain, fussilat offers a specific methodology of explanation: breaking things apart and dealing with them one at a time so there’s no ambiguity or confusion. Allah ﷻ says the ayahs have been broken apart, taken one matter at a time for complete clarity.

قُرْءَانًا عَرَبِيًّا—an Arabic recital—for a nation that wants to learn, for a people who want to know.

Ayah 4

بَشِيرًا وَنَذِيرًا فَأَعْرَضَ أَكْثَرُهُمْ فَهُمْ لَا يَسْمَعُونَ

Notice something powerful here: بَشِيرًا وَنَذِيرًا—a giver of good news and a warner—is usually how we describe the Prophet ﷺ himself. But here, it describes the Quran. There’s no difference being made between the Messenger and the Message. The Prophet ﷺ is basheer and natheer. The Quran is basheer and natheer. The Quran gives good news and gives warning.

فَأَعْرَضَ أَكْثَرُهُمْ—then most of them deliberately ignored it. They turned away with intention. فَهُمْ لَا يَسْمَعُونَ—then they refused to listen. They’re just not going to listen.

This sets up a powerful historical moment. Utbah ibn Rabee’ah, a great leader of the Quraysh and one of their finest poets, was sent to confront the Prophet ﷺ. He came and started his assault: “You’ve caused humiliation to the Arabs, to the entire race. Everyone makes fun of us now because of you. You’ve caused separation between father and son.” Then he started negotiating: “What are you looking for? Power? We’ll give you leadership. Money? We’ll have a fundraiser and gather wealth for you. Women? We’ll get you ten women to marry—you pick. Just end this.”

He kept unleashing his arguments at the Prophet ﷺ, and the Prophet ﷺ let him finish completely. When he was done, the Prophet ﷺ asked, “أَفَرَغْتَ؟”—“Are you done pouring out? Is your container empty now?”

Utbah said yes, he was ready to listen. The Prophet ﷺ began reciting this very Surah: “Ha Meem. Tanzeelun minar-Rahmanir-Raheem…”

Ayah 5

وَقَالُوا۟ قُلُوبُنَا فِىٓ أَكِنَّةٍ مِّمَّا تَدْعُونَآ إِلَيْهِ وَفِىٓ ءَاذَانِنَا وَقْرٌ وَمِنۢ بَيْنِنَا وَبَيْنِكَ حِجَابٌ فَٱعْمَلْ إِنَّنَا عَـٰمِلُونَ

They would say, “Our hearts are in أَكِنَّةٍ—in vaults, in protective coverings, layers upon layers blocking us from what you’re calling us to. We’ve sealed our hearts away in a mountain vault, unreachable. وَفِىٓ ءَاذَانِنَا وَقْرٌ—and in our ears there’s a barrier, a filter.” Waqr suggests a cork placed in the ear—a psychological plug preventing any sound from penetrating.

وَمِنۢ بَيْنِنَا وَبَيْنِكَ حِجَابٌ—“And between us and you, there’s a barrier, a line that cannot be crossed.”

فَٱعْمَلْ إِنَّنَا عَـٰمِلُونَ—“So do whatever you want. We are going to do whatever we want.” In other words, the Prophet ﷺ is being directly challenged. They’re saying, “We see you’re active every day with your message. Well, we’re going to be equally active every day to undo everything you’re doing. We’re going to try to bring you down, to destroy your mission.”

Ayah 6

قُلْ إِنَّمَآ أَنَا۠ بَشَرٌ مِّثْلُكُمْ يُوحَىٰٓ إِلَىَّ أَنَّمَآ إِلَـٰهُكُمْ إِلَـٰهٌ وَٰحِدٌ فَٱسْتَقِيمُوٓا۟ إِلَيْهِ وَٱسْتَغْفِرُوهُ ۗ وَوَيْلٌ لِّلْمُشْرِكِينَ

Tell them: “I am a human being no different than yourselves. يُوحَىٰٓ إِلَىَّ—revelation has been given to me—أَنَّمَآ إِلَـٰهُكُمْ إِلَـٰهٌ وَٰحِدٌ—that your God, your object of worship and obedience, is One.”

فَٱسْتَقِيمُوٓا۟ إِلَيْهِ—then be straight and upright toward Him. Deal with Him directly. The word اسْتَقِيمُوا comes from قَامَ, which means to stand. اِسْتَقَامَ means to try your best to stand straight, to do your utmost to stand without bending or wavering to this side or that. It also means to commit yourself to standing, to hold your ground, fighting exhaustion, fighting laziness, fighting whatever tries to move you from that position.

وَٱسْتَغْفِرُوهُ—and seek His forgiveness.

وَوَيْلٌ لِّلْمُشْرِكِينَ—and the worst destruction falls upon those who commit shirk.

Ayah 7

ٱلَّذِينَ لَا يُؤْتُونَ ٱلزَّكَوٰةَ وَهُم بِٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ هُمْ كَـٰفِرُونَ

Those who don’t give Az-Zakah—the purification. Some scholars note that since this is a Makkan revelation, this may not be referring specifically to the 2.5% Zakah obligation revealed in Madinah, but rather the broader concept of giving as a means of spiritual cleansing. The idea that purifying your wealth purifies your soul was already taught in Makkah.

وَهُم بِٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ هُمْ كَـٰفِرُونَ—and those kinds of people, especially when it comes to the afterlife, they are in complete denial.

Ayah 8

إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ لَهُمْ أَجْرٌ غَيْرُ مَمْنُونٍ

No doubt, those who believed and acted righteously—لَهُمْ أَجْرٌ غَيْرُ مَمْنُونٍ—they will have compensation that doesn’t come to an end. The word مَمْنُونٍ comes from مَنَّ, which means to cut off, to discontinue. So غَيْرُ مَمْنُونٍ means unending, never discontinued, perpetual.

Ayah 9

۞ قُلْ أَئِنَّكُمْ لَتَكْفُرُونَ بِٱلَّذِى خَلَقَ ٱلْأَرْضَ فِى يَوْمَيْنِ وَتَجْعَلُونَ لَهُۥٓ أَندَادًا ۚ ذَٰلِكَ رَبُّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ

Tell them: Are you really disbelieving? Are you absolutely certain you’re rejecting the One Who created the Earth in two days? وَتَجْعَلُونَ لَهُۥٓ أَندَادًا—and then you put partners and competitors alongside Him?

The word نِدّ, plural أَندَاد, means a competitor, someone who’s neck-and-neck with you. In sports, when two cars are racing and you don’t know which will win because they’re right there together—that’s andaad, equivalent competitors. You put people who are equivalent competitors with Allah ﷻ?

ذَٰلِكَ رَبُّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ—That is the Master of all nations, all peoples, all worlds. Do you realize what you’ve done?

Ayah 10

وَجَعَلَ فِيهَا رَوَٰسِىَ مِن فَوْقِهَا وَبَـٰرَكَ فِيهَا وَقَدَّرَ فِيهَآ أَقْوَٰتَهَا فِىٓ أَرْبَعَةِ أَيَّامٍ سَوَآءً لِّلسَّآئِلِينَ

He placed in the earth رَوَٰسِىَ—mountains—مِن فَوْقِهَا—from above it. Mountains were sent down, just as revelation was sent down. Allah ﷻ tells us this in Surah Al-Hadeed as well.

وَبَـٰرَكَ فِيهَا—and He placed barakah in it. He put the power of increase, the power of growth inside the mountains and the earth, meaning the earth has the potential to vegetate, to grow, to produce life.

وَقَدَّرَ فِيهَآ أَقْوَٰتَهَا—and He set in it أَقْوَات, plural of قُوت. Qoot is the kind of sustenance that maintains the life of an entire environment. If it disappears, everything dies. When a pond dries up, the fish die. When the fish die, the animals that fed on them begin to die. When those animals die, the plants that depended on that ecosystem start dying. Each environment has its qoot—its life-sustaining food supply.

If you study different habitats in nature documentaries, you see how each provides for certain insects, animals and plants. Each has its food supply arranged for it. Each has exactly what it needs to survive. This is قُوت. Allah ﷻ set up this entire mechanism.

فِىٓ أَرْبَعَةِ أَيَّامٍ—in four additional days. So two days for creating the Earth itself, then four days for the mountains and the food systems.

سَوَآءً لِّلسَّآئِلِينَ—made equally available for all those who look around. The one who searches for food, the one looking around for sustenance—they will find something to eat.

Ayah 11

ثُمَّ ٱسْتَوَىٰٓ إِلَى ٱلسَّمَآءِ وَهِىَ دُخَانٌ فَقَالَ لَهَا وَلِلْأَرْضِ ٱئْتِيَا طَوْعًا أَوْ كَرْهًا قَالَتَآ أَتَيْنَا طَآئِعِينَ

Then He turned His attention to the sky—وَهِىَ دُخَانٌ—and it was smoke. دُخَان refers to what rises from fire, the parts of something burning that haven’t completely burned down yet, still releasing smoke.

فَقَالَ لَهَا وَلِلْأَرْضِ—then He said to it and to the Earth: ٱئْتِيَا—come! Submit! Move! The command ٱئْتِيَا means both to come and to move into motion. Some scholars suggest Allah ﷻ commanded the sky and earth to get into motion, to start moving. And to this day, everything in the physical universe is in motion—every atom, every subatomic particle, every planet, every star, every galaxy is in constant motion.

طَوْعًا أَوْ كَرْهًا—willingly or unwillingly.

قَالَتَآ أَتَيْنَا طَآئِعِينَ—they both said, “We come before You in complete submission, in complete willingness.”

Ayah 12

فَقَضَىٰهُنَّ سَبْعَ سَمَـٰوَاتٍ فِى يَوْمَيْنِ وَأَوْحَىٰ فِى كُلِّ سَمَآءٍ أَمْرَهَا ۚ وَزَيَّنَّا ٱلسَّمَآءَ ٱلدُّنْيَا بِمَصَـٰبِيحَ وَحِفْظًا ۚ ذَٰلِكَ تَقْدِيرُ ٱلْعَزِيزِ ٱلْعَلِيمِ

Thus He declared them to be seven skies فِى يَوْمَيْنِ—in two days. There could be overlap between these days and the days mentioned for the Earth. Just as you might work on two projects simultaneously—one takes two days, another takes three days, but that doesn’t mean five total days because they overlap—so too, the two days for the Earth could overlap with the two days for the sky.

But notice: Allah ﷻ took special care describing the Earth—two days, then another four days. When He talked about the rest of the universe, He mentioned it was done quickly in two days. There are special qualities of this planet that are unique. Allah ﷻ placed us on a planet that received particular attention. When something is given more time in the description, it’s been given more attention in reality.

وَأَوْحَىٰ فِى كُلِّ سَمَآءٍ أَمْرَهَا—then He inspired and instructed to every single one of those layers of sky its own command. Each sky has its own set of instructions, its own function. The first sky, for example, has the instruction to fire away at the jinn who try to steal revelation or information from the angels—the intergalactic battle happening in the skies that we’ve read about in other passages.

وَزَيَّنَّا ٱلسَّمَآءَ ٱلدُّنْيَا بِمَصَـٰبِيحَ—and We beautified the lowest sky with stars—وَحِفْظًا—and as a means of security. We’ve learned about this protective function previously.

ذَٰلِكَ تَقْدِيرُ ٱلْعَزِيزِ ٱلْعَلِيمِ—that is the calculation and decree of the Ultimate Authority, the One Who knows everything.

Ayah 13

فَإِنْ أَعْرَضُوا۟ فَقُلْ أَنذَرْتُكُمْ صَـٰعِقَةً مِّثْلَ صَـٰعِقَةِ عَادٍ وَثَمُودَ

Now we return to the conversation between the Prophet ﷺ and Utbah. Allah ﷻ is still speaking, but these are words the Prophet ﷺ is delivering. This is the final part of the Prophet’s speech to Utbah.

فَإِنْ أَعْرَضُوا۟—then if they still continue to ignore—فَقُلْ—then say: أَنذَرْتُكُمْ صَـٰعِقَةً—I am warning you of an explosion, a sound that will tear you apart—مِّثْلَ صَـٰعِقَةِ عَادٍ وَثَمُودَ—like the devastating blast that seized ’Ad and Thamud.

Here’s what’s remarkable: two people are talking—the Prophet ﷺ is talking to Utbah. One gives a speech saying “I’m talking to you,” but then the second person doesn’t talk back. Allah ﷻ talks back. And the Prophet ﷺ keeps reiterating that these aren’t his words. Who is قُلْ being said to? To the Prophet ﷺ. But who is actually speaking? Allah ﷻ.

When Utbah heard this ayah—when he heard about the saa’iqah—he lunged forward and grabbed the mouth of the Prophet ﷺ. “No more! No more! I beg of you by the ties of kinship, have mercy!”

Was he asking the Prophet ﷺ for mercy? No. In those moments, even Utbah understood—he was asking Allah ﷻ for mercy.

For several days after this, the Quraysh didn’t see Utbah. When they finally went to his house, they found him depressed, staying indoors. They suspected, “Maybe Muhammad gave him food or helped him financially, and now he feels indebted and doesn’t want to speak against him.”

He grew angry at their suggestion. He was one of the wealthiest people in Makkah and one of the most eloquent speakers. “I didn’t say anything back because I didn’t want any calamity to come to us.” That’s one narration. In another narration, he told them, “Whatever that man has to say, it’s going to be huge. It’s going to happen. I just walked away. I didn’t want to deal with it.” He was completely disheveled from this encounter.

Ayah 14

إِذْ جَآءَتْهُمُ ٱلرُّسُلُ مِنۢ بَيْنِ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَمِنْ خَلْفِهِمْ أَلَّا تَعْبُدُوٓا۟ إِلَّا ٱللَّهَ ۖ قَالُوا۟ لَوْ شَآءَ رَبُّنَا لَأَنزَلَ مَلَـٰٓئِكَةً فَإِنَّا بِمَآ أُرْسِلْتُم بِهِۦ كَـٰفِرُونَ

When the messengers came to them—مِنۢ بَيْنِ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَمِنْ خَلْفِهِمْ—from right in front of them and from behind them. In other words, publicly and privately. The messengers would constantly come to them. They would skip no opportunity to speak to them with the message: أَلَّا تَعْبُدُوٓا۟ إِلَّا ٱللَّهَ—that you should not enslave yourselves to anyone other than Allah ﷻ.

قَالُوا۟ لَوْ شَآءَ رَبُّنَا لَأَنزَلَ مَلَـٰٓئِكَةً—they would respond: “If our Master wanted, He would have sent down angels. You’re not that impressive. You’re just human beings. You just said yourself you’re بَشَرٌ like us.”

فَإِنَّا بِمَآ أُرْسِلْتُم بِهِۦ كَـٰفِرُونَ—“And we are in complete denial of whatever you think you’ve been sent with”—their sarcastic, mocking tone preserved in the Quran.

“We’re not going to believe this. Bring us the angel himself.”

Ayah 15

فَأَمَّا عَادٌ فَٱسْتَكْبَرُوا۟ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ بِغَيْرِ ٱلْحَقِّ وَقَالُوا۟ مَنْ أَشَدُّ مِنَّا قُوَّةً ۖ أَوَلَمْ يَرَوْا۟ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَهُمْ هُوَ أَشَدُّ مِنْهُمْ قُوَّةً ۖ وَكَانُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا يَجْحَدُونَ

As for the people of ’Ad, فَٱسْتَكْبَرُوا۟ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ بِغَيْرِ ٱلْحَقِّ—they showed arrogance in the land without any justification.

وَقَالُوا۟ مَنْ أَشَدُّ مِنَّا قُوَّةً—they looked around and said, “Who is more powerful than us? Who’s tougher than us in might?”

أَوَلَمْ يَرَوْا۟ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَهُمْ هُوَ أَشَدُّ مِنْهُمْ قُوَّةً—didn’t they ever think about the fact that Allah ﷻ, the One Who created them, is more powerful than they are?

They said, “Who’s more powerful than us?” Allah ﷻ responds: Okay, you can’t find a nation more powerful than you, so you forget that I am more powerful than you?

وَكَانُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا يَجْحَدُونَ—and knowing full well that Allah ﷻ is more powerful, they would still argue, debate and rebel against His miraculous signs and revelations.

Ayah 16

فَأَرْسَلْنَا عَلَيْهِمْ رِيحًا صَرْصَرًا فِىٓ أَيَّامٍ نَّحِسَاتٍ لِّنُذِيقَهُمْ عَذَابَ ٱلْخِزْىِ فِى ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا ۖ وَلَعَذَابُ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ أَخْزَىٰ ۖ وَهُمْ لَا يُنصَرُونَ

فَأَرْسَلْنَا عَلَيْهِمْ رِيحًا صَرْصَرًا—then We sent against them a wind, صَرْصَر.

The root صَرّ means intense cold, like freezing rain. Sarsar is the intensified form—a killer wind. If you’ve experienced winter in Chicago, Boston, parts of Ohio or Michigan, Minnesota—when it’s not just cold but viciously windy, and you step outside just to scrape ice off your car and your ear feels like it’s about to fall off—that’s the kind of cold that can kill you. There are people in Colorado and other states who die from exposure to freezing wind alone. This is sarsar—a wind so cold it can freeze you to death.

Allah ﷻ gives us a parallel in Surah Aal-’Imran, describing a freezing wind that attacks a farm. The plants freeze and simply break off and shatter. That’s صَرّ. Sarsar is the hyperbolic, extreme form of this devastating cold.

فِىٓ أَيَّامٍ نَّحِسَاتٍ—in wretched days, evil days, days of misfortune.

لِّنُذِيقَهُمْ عَذَابَ ٱلْخِزْىِ فِى ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا—so that We could make them taste the punishment of disgrace in the life of this world.

وَلَعَذَابُ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ أَخْزَىٰ—and surely the punishment of the Hereafter is even more disgracing.

وَهُمْ لَا يُنصَرُونَ—and they will have no one to help them, no one to save them.

Ayah 16–25 · linked video: 02. Fussilat (Ayah 16-25)

Surah Fussilat: Ayahs 16-25

Ayah 16

فَأَرْسَلْنَا عَلَيْهِمْ رِيحًا صَرْصَرًا فِىٓ أَيَّامٍ نَّحِسَاتٍ لِّنُذِيقَهُمْ عَذَابَ ٱلْخِزْىِ فِى ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا ۖ وَلَعَذَابُ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ أَخْزَىٰ ۖ وَهُمْ لَا يُنصَرُونَ

The ayah describes the punishment that befell the people of ’Ad. Allah ﷻ says He sent upon them رِيحًا صَرْصَرًا- a freezing, violent wind. The word صَرْصَر carries the intensity of extreme cold, the kind of cold that penetrates to the bone. This wasn’t merely uncomfortable weather; this was a devastating force of nature unleashed as divine punishment.

The phrase فِىٓ أَيَّامٍ نَّحِسَاتٍ- “in days of misfortune” - indicates a period of continuous torment. The word نَحِسَات suggests days of bad luck, one after another. Linguistically, أَيَّام in the plural but limited form suggests less than ten days. Later in the Quran, we learn this punishment lasted seven nights and eight days continuously.

Consider the nature of this punishment: these were people who boasted of their power and strength. Allah ﷻ essentially challenged them - you claim such power? Try fighting against the wind. For eight days straight, they were hammered by this subfreezing tornado. When things freeze, they become brittle. Imagine a hurricane combined with subfreezing temperatures - objects smashing into each other and shattering from the cold and impact. This is the reality of صَرْصَر.

The purpose is stated: لِّنُذِيقَهُمْ عَذَابَ ٱلْخِزْىِ - “so We could make them taste the punishment of humiliation.” The word خِزْي specifically means humiliation. Why humiliation? Because they had placed their trust in their own strength and power. Now they stood powerless before a force of nature, completely unable to defend themselves or their people.

This punishment occurred فِى ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا - in the worldly life. But Allah ﷻ adds: وَلَعَذَابُ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ أَخْزَىٰ - “and the punishment of the Hereafter is even more humiliating.” Whatever they experienced in this world, no matter how severe, pales in comparison to what awaits in the next life for those who reject Allah’s message.

The ayah concludes: وَهُمْ لَا يُنصَرُونَ - “and they will not be helped.” No one could save them from this wind, and no one will be able to save them from the punishment of the Hereafter. This is a pattern we see throughout the Quran: when Allah’s punishment comes, there are no helpers, no protectors, no escape routes.

Ayah 17

وَأَمَّا ثَمُودُ فَهَدَيْنَـٰهُمْ فَٱسْتَحَبُّوا۟ ٱلْعَمَىٰ عَلَى ٱلْهُدَىٰ فَأَخَذَتْهُمْ صَـٰعِقَةُ ٱلْعَذَابِ ٱلْهُونِ بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَكْسِبُونَ

Now the focus shifts to the people of Thamud. Allah ﷻ says: وَأَمَّا ثَمُودُ فَهَدَيْنَـٰهُمْ - “As for Thamud, We guided them.” This is significant. Allah ﷻ didn’t leave them in confusion or doubt. The message was crystal clear. The prophet Salih (AS) came to them with undeniable signs, including the miraculous she-camel. They had guidance laid out before them with absolute clarity.

But then comes the devastating statement: فَٱسْتَحَبُّوا۟ ٱلْعَمَىٰ عَلَى ٱلْهُدَىٰ - “but they preferred blindness over guidance.” The word اسْتَحَبُّوا comes from the root meaning “love.” They actively loved and chose blindness. They saw the truth, recognized it, and then deliberately turned away from it. They preferred to remain in their state of heedlessness rather than accept the guidance that was offered.

This is a profound psychological reality. Sometimes people are presented with absolute truth, but the lifestyle change it demands is too much for them. They know what’s right, but they choose what’s comfortable. They see reality but prefer the illusion because accepting the truth means accountability, means change, means giving up certain comforts or relationships or habits.

The consequence of this choice: فَأَخَذَتْهُمْ صَـٰعِقَةُ ٱلْعَذَابِ ٱلْهُونِ - “so the blast of humiliating punishment seized them.” The word صَاعِقَة refers to a thunderous explosion or blast. Combined with هُون - another word for humiliation - this describes a punishment that strips away all dignity and pride.

The word هُون is particularly powerful. It encompasses being diminished in people’s eyes, being considered worthless, being rendered powerless. All of these meanings converge in this punishment. The ayah ends: بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَكْسِبُونَ - “because of what they used to earn.” Their destruction wasn’t arbitrary; it was the direct result of their choices and actions.

Ayah 18

وَنَجَّيْنَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَكَانُوا۟ يَتَّقُونَ

This brief but beautiful ayah provides the contrast: وَنَجَّيْنَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَكَانُوا۟ يَتَّقُونَ - “And We rescued those who believed and were mindful of Allah.”

The word نَجَّيْنَا means to rescue or save from danger. While the majority faced destruction, those who believed weren’t swept up in the collective punishment. Their faith and their تَقْوَىٰ - their consciousness of Allah, their protective caution - saved them.

Notice the two characteristics mentioned: ءَامَنُوا۟ - they believed, and كَانُوا۟ يَتَّقُونَ - they continuously practiced taqwa. The verb كَانُوا indicates an ongoing state. These weren’t people who simply claimed belief; they lived it through constant mindfulness of Allah ﷻ. This combination of faith and righteous caution is what earned them Allah’s protection when His punishment descended on their people.

Ayah 19

وَيَوْمَ يُحْشَرُ أَعْدَآءُ ٱللَّهِ إِلَى ٱلنَّارِ فَهُمْ يُوزَعُونَ

The scene now transitions to the Day of Judgment. Allah ﷻ says: وَيَوْمَ يُحْشَرُ أَعْدَآءُ ٱللَّهِ إِلَى ٱلنَّارِ - “And the Day when the enemies of Allah will be gathered toward the Fire.”

The word يُحْشَرُ means to be herded or gathered together, the way livestock are gathered. These are described as أَعْدَآءُ ٱللَّهِ - “enemies of Allah.” This is a powerful designation. Through their rejection and opposition to His message, through their persecution of believers, they made themselves enemies of Allah ﷻ.

Then comes the description: فَهُمْ يُوزَعُونَ - “then they will be organized into groups.” The word يُوزَعُونَ comes from the root meaning to distribute or organize into regiments. This same word was used in the Quran to describe how the army of Sulayman (AS) was organized-regiments of jinn, regiments of humans, regiments of birds, all perfectly arranged.

Here, the disbelievers will be organized and divided into groups as they’re driven toward the Fire. There’s an order to it, a system. This isn’t chaos; this is divine justice being executed with precision.

Ayah 20

حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا مَا جَآءُوهَا شَهِدَ عَلَيْهِمْ سَمْعُهُمْ وَأَبْصَـٰرُهُمْ وَجُلُودُهُم بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ

The march continues: حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا مَا جَآءُوهَا - “until when they reach it.” They arrive at the Fire they were being driven toward. But what happens next is extraordinary: شَهِدَ عَلَيْهِمْ سَمْعُهُمْ وَأَبْصَـٰرُهُمْ وَجُلُودُهُم - “their hearing, their sight, and their skins will testify against them.”

Imagine standing in a court of law looking for witnesses to testify against you, and suddenly your own body parts begin to speak. Your ears start talking. Your eyes begin giving testimony. Your skin begins to recount what it witnessed. The very instruments through which you committed sins now become witnesses against you.

They testify بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ - “about what they used to do.” Every unlawful glance, every forbidden conversation, every impermissible touch - all of it recorded, all of it remembered, all of it now being testified to by the very body parts that participated in those actions.

This changes our entire relationship with our own bodies. These ears that might be tempted to listen to backbiting, these eyes that might be drawn to look at what’s forbidden, this skin that might be about to touch something impermissible - they’re all going to be witnesses. There’s an accountability built into our very physical form.

Ayah 21

وَقَالُوا۟ لِجُلُودِهِمْ لِمَ شَهِدتُّمْ عَلَيْنَا ۖ قَالُوٓا۟ أَنطَقَنَا ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِىٓ أَنطَقَ كُلَّ شَىْءٍ وَهُوَ خَلَقَكُمْ أَوَّلَ مَرَّةٍ وَإِلَيْهِ تُرْجَعُونَ

The scene becomes even more surreal. These people turn to their own skin in protest: وَقَالُوا۟ لِجُلُودِهِمْ لِمَ شَهِدتُّمْ عَلَيْنَا - “They will say to their skins, ‘Why have you testified against us?’”

This is the reaction of betrayal. We’re one team! You’re my skin! How could you testify against me? There’s an expectation that your body should have loyalty to you, should cover for you, should keep your secrets. But that’s not how it works.

The skin responds: قَالُوٓا۟ أَنطَقَنَا ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِىٓ أَنطَقَ كُلَّ شَىْءٍ - “They will say, ‘Allah has made us speak, He Who has given speech to everything.’”

This is profound. The skin acknowledges that Allah ﷻ is the One Who gave it the ability to speak. But more than that - He’s the One Who gave everything its form of speech. Every created thing has a way of communicating, of declaring Allah’s glory. We mentioned before the ayah: “There is nothing except that it glorifies Allah with praise, but you don’t understand their glorification.” (17:44)

The mountains sang along with Dawud (AS). The birds had their own form of praising Allah ﷻ. Everything in creation has speech; we just don’t comprehend most of it. Today, on the Day of Judgment, we’ll understand the speech of our own body parts.

The skin continues: وَهُوَ خَلَقَكُمْ أَوَّلَ مَرَّةٍ - “And He created you the first time.” This is a reminder of Allah’s creative power. He engineered you once before, gave you life, gave you consciousness, gave you the ability to speak and act.

The implication is clear: if He could create you the first time, He can certainly recreate you in a different form. On the Day of Judgment, there will be a version 2.0 of humanity where the rules are different, where body parts can testify, where the physical form operates according to different laws.

The ayah concludes: وَإِلَيْهِ تُرْجَعُونَ - “and to Him you are being returned.” Not “you will be returned” but “you are being returned” - using the present tense to indicate the certainty and immediacy of this return. This isn’t speculation; this is reality.

Ayah 22

وَمَا كُنتُمْ تَسْتَتِرُونَ أَن يَشْهَدَ عَلَيْكُمْ سَمْعُكُمْ وَلَآ أَبْصَـٰرُكُمْ وَلَا جُلُودُكُمْ وَلَـٰكِن ظَنَنتُمْ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يَعْلَمُ كَثِيرًا مِّمَّا تَعْمَلُونَ

Now Allah ﷻ reveals their underlying mindset: وَمَا كُنتُمْ تَسْتَتِرُونَ أَن يَشْهَدَ عَلَيْكُمْ سَمْعُكُمْ وَلَآ أَبْصَـٰرُكُمْ وَلَا جُلُودُكُمْ - “You were not hiding yourselves from the testimony of your hearing, your sight, or your skins.”

The word تَسْتَتِرُونَ means to cover something up completely, to hide it thoroughly. Allah ﷻ is saying: you never thought about hiding from your own body parts. You never considered that your ears, eyes, and skin would be witnesses. That never crossed your mind as something to worry about.

So what was the real issue? وَلَـٰكِن ظَنَنتُمْ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يَعْلَمُ كَثِيرًا مِّمَّا تَعْمَلُونَ - “But rather, you assumed that Allah does not know much of what you do.”

This is the core delusion. They had a fundamentally flawed understanding of Allah ﷻ. They thought He was too busy with bigger matters to pay attention to their individual actions. Maybe He knows some things, but certainly not everything. Certainly not the small sins, the private moments, the things done in secret.

This kind of thinking has infected various groups throughout history. Some philosophers developed concepts of God as a distant, detached being who set creation in motion but doesn’t involve Himself in its details. They imagine God as someone who kicked a ball and now it’s just rolling on its own - He’s moved on to other things.

Tragically, such ideas have even influenced some Muslims who became enamored with philosophy. They study philosophical texts extensively until those ideas become deeply embedded in their thinking. Then, when they approach the Quran, they try to make it conform to their pre-existing philosophical framework rather than letting the Quran shape their understanding.

These same people will read ayahs about the body parts testifying and dismiss them as metaphorical language meant to scare simple people into good behavior. They consider themselves too sophisticated for such “primitive” motivations. But this ayah exposes the reality: their assumption that Allah ﷻ doesn’t know much of what they do is precisely what will lead to their destruction.

The truth that the Quran establishes is that Allah ﷻ knows يَعْلَمُ خَائِنَةَ ٱلْأَعْيُنِ (the stealing glance of the eyes) (40:19). He knows even that subtle, fleeting look. How can anyone assume He doesn’t know about the apps on their phone, the websites they visit, the conversations they have?

Ayah 23

وَذَٰلِكُمْ ظَنُّكُمُ ٱلَّذِى ظَنَنتُم بِرَبِّكُمْ أَرْدَىٰكُمْ فَأَصْبَحْتُم مِّنَ ٱلْخَـٰسِرِينَ

Allah ﷻ now identifies the root cause of their downfall: وَذَٰلِكُمْ ظَنُّكُمُ ٱلَّذِى ظَنَنتُم بِرَبِّكُمْ أَرْدَىٰكُمْ - “And that assumption of yours which you assumed about your Lord - it destroyed you.”

The word أَرْدَىٰ means to throw someone off a cliff, to cause them to fall to destruction. This same word appears in Surah Al-Kahf when one friend says to another: “Perhaps your Lord will give you better than his garden, and send upon it a thunderbolt from the sky, and it will become smooth ground, or its water will sink into the earth so you would never be able to find it.” (18:40) The word used there is أَرْدَى - causing destruction, throwing into ruin.

These baseless assumptions about Allah ﷻ - thinking He doesn’t know, thinking He doesn’t care, thinking He’s too busy with bigger matters - these very thoughts are what threw them off the cliff of guidance into the abyss of destruction.

The consequence: فَأَصْبَحْتُم مِّنَ ٱلْخَـٰسِرِينَ - “and you became from among the losers.” The word أَصْبَحْ literally means morning, because morning transforms the night into day. It’s used to indicate transformation or becoming something different.

The benefit of this word choice is significant: فَأَصْبَحْتُم - “you became” - implies they weren’t losers to begin with. They had the opportunity. They had guidance available to them. They had the Quran. They had the truth presented clearly. But they allowed their philosophical assumptions, their baseless ideas about Allah ﷻ, to override the revelation. They wouldn’t let the Quran clean out their misconceptions and replace them with the truth. That transformation - from people with potential to confirmed losers - was their own doing.

This is one of the greatest benefits of the Quran: it gives us the correct understanding of who Allah ﷻ is and what He expects from us. At the most basic level, studying the Quran should develop in us the right attitude about Allah ﷻ, about His attributes, about how He deals with His creation, about what kinds of people should have hope in Him and what kinds should worry about their standing before Him.

Ayah 24

فَإِن يَصْبِرُوا۟ فَٱلنَّارُ مَثْوًى لَّهُمْ ۖ وَإِن يَسْتَعْتِبُوا۟ فَمَا هُم مِّنَ ٱلْمُعْتَبِينَ

The finality of their situation is now made clear: فَإِن يَصْبِرُوا۟ فَٱلنَّارُ مَثْوًى لَّهُمْ - “Then if they endure patiently, the Fire is a residence for them.”

Even if they try to bear it with patience, even if they try to endure, it makes no difference. The Fire will be their مَثْوًى - their dwelling place, their residence for eons upon eons. The word mathwa implies a place where one stays for a long time, generation after generation.

Then comes an even more hopeless scenario: وَإِن يَسْتَعْتِبُوا۟ فَمَا هُم مِّنَ ٱلْمُعْتَبِينَ - “And if they ask to be excused, they will not be of those who are excused.”

The word عَتَبَ means to point out someone’s faults or mistakes, to offer correction. When you tell your brother or friend, “What you did wasn’t right, you shouldn’t have done that” - that’s ’ataba. There’s an Arabic expression: “To correct your friend is better than losing your friend.”

The word يَسْتَعْتِبُوا means to ask that their crimes not be held against them, to request that their wrongs be overlooked or excused. But Allah ﷻ says: فَمَا هُم مِّنَ ٱلْمُعْتَبِينَ - they won’t be from those who are given any excuse, whose wrongs are overlooked.

The root word also relates to the image of a chick breaking out of an eggshell - that first struggle, that first exercise that prepares it for life. If someone else breaks the shell for the chick, it’s actually harmful because the chick needs that struggle to develop its muscles. The time for that preparation, that correction, that opportunity to make things right - that time has passed. There are no more chances, no more excuses, no more opportunities for redemption.

Ayah 25

۞ وَقَيَّضْنَا لَهُمْ قُرَنَآءَ فَزَيَّنُوا۟ لَهُم مَّا بَيْنَ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَمَا خَلْفَهُمْ وَحَقَّ عَلَيْهِمُ ٱلْقَوْلُ فِىٓ أُمَمٍ قَدْ خَلَتْ مِن قَبْلِهِم مِّنَ ٱلْجِنِّ وَٱلْإِنسِ ۖ إِنَّهُمْ كَانُوا۟ خَـٰسِرِينَ

Finally, Allah ﷻ reveals another dimension of their story: وَقَيَّضْنَا لَهُمْ قُرَنَآءَ - “And We assigned to them companions.”

The word قَيَّضْنَا means to hatch, to prepare, to assign for a task. The word قُرَنَآءَ comes from قَرِين, meaning one who is joined or linked to another. When prisoners are chained together, they’re called مُقَرَّنِين.

Just as Allah ﷻ has assigned angels to document our deeds, just as He has guardian angels protecting us, He has also allowed for the existence of a شَيْطَان assigned to each person - our قَرِين. This being spends their entire existence with us. They know our weaknesses intimately. They know what tempts us, what makes us lose our composure, what causes us to give in to our desires, what makes us consume the forbidden. They know exactly where our vulnerabilities lie.

And when they find the opportunity, they push and push and push. This is what the ayah describes next: فَزَيَّنُوا۟ لَهُم مَّا بَيْنَ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَمَا خَلْفَهُمْ - “and they beautified for them what was before them and what was behind them.”

مَا بَيْنَ أَيْدِيهِمْ - what’s immediately in front of them - refers to the temptations and desires that are right before their eyes. The sequel to that show just came out - how can you possibly not watch it? Everyone’s going to that concert - you’ll be the only one who missed out! That opportunity won’t come again - you have to seize it now!

Young people especially experience this when begging their parents to go somewhere impermissible. They act as though their life will end if they don’t get to do this thing. It’s the qareen behind the scenes, working on them, making it seem like missing out on this will be catastrophic. It’s overdramatic obsession with something that comes from this constant companion who beautifies what’s in front of them.

وَمَا خَلْفَهُمْ - what’s behind them - refers to beautifying their past track record. The qareen makes your past sins look justified, makes your wrong choices seem reasonable at the time, makes your history of disobedience appear less severe than it actually was. They beautify both your future temptations and your past mistakes.

The consequence of this companionship: وَحَقَّ عَلَيْهِمُ ٱلْقَوْلُ - “And the decree became inevitable upon them.” The word حَقَّ means it became due, it became certain, it became justified. The decree of punishment, the judgment against them - it became an established reality.

This happened فِىٓ أُمَمٍ قَدْ خَلَتْ مِن قَبْلِهِم مِّنَ ٱلْجِنِّ وَٱلْإِنسِ - “among nations that had passed before them of jinn and humans.” They’re not unique in their fate. Many nations before them, both jinn and human, followed the same path and met the same end.

The ayah concludes with the same description we saw earlier: إِنَّهُمْ كَانُوا۟ خَـٰسِرِينَ - “Indeed, they were losers.” They had every opportunity but threw it all away by following their constant companions into destruction rather than following the guidance of Allah ﷻ.

Ayah 25–31 · linked video: 03. Fussilat (Ayah 25-31)

Surah Fussilat: Ayahs 25-31

Ayah 25

۞ وَقَيَّضْنَا لَهُمْ قُرَنَآءَ فَزَيَّنُوا۟ لَهُم مَّا بَيْنَ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَمَا خَلْفَهُمْ وَحَقَّ عَلَيْهِمُ ٱلْقَوْلُ فِىٓ أُمَمٍ قَدْ خَلَتْ مِن قَبْلِهِم مِّنَ ٱلْجِنِّ وَٱلْإِنسِ ۖ إِنَّهُمْ كَانُوا۟ خَـٰسِرِينَ

The word قَيَّضْنَا carries a deep meaning: Allah ﷻ assigns companions to certain people as a consequence of their choices. These قُرَنَآءَ, or constant companions, have a specific mission: to beautify both what lies ahead of them and what lies behind them. This beautification operates in two dimensions of time and reality.

The qarīn beautifies your past to you. When you look back at your track record, you don’t see anything terribly wrong. You think to yourself, “I’m not that bad. I haven’t done anything seriously wrong.” You see someone crying in prayer, begging Allah ﷻ for forgiveness, and you wonder what terrible sins they must have committed. Meanwhile, you say istighfar as a mere formality, confident that you’re already forgiven. This is the work of the qarīn—making your past appear acceptable so you feel no urgency to seek genuine forgiveness. If you don’t worry about your past, what reason do you have to make sincere tawbah?

The qarīn also beautifies what lies ahead. Your future looks fine, your path seems clear, and there appears to be no need for course correction. This companion becomes your personal enemy, assigned specifically to you by Shaytan himself. Just as Adam (AS) had Shaytan as his personal adversary, every human being receives their own personal devil—a qarīn whose sole mission is to lead them astray.

The ayah continues: وَحَقَّ عَلَيْهِمُ ٱلْقَوْلُ—“the word manifested on them.” The verdict of Allah ﷻ became deserving upon them. But notice the scope: فِىٓ أُمَمٍ قَدْ خَلَتْ مِن قَبْلِهِم مِّنَ ٱلْجِنِّ وَٱلْإِنسِ—among nations that have already passed, from both jinn and human beings. Every qarīn assigned to a human being shares their ultimate fate. When a person’s mission ends, when their life concludes, the verdict doesn’t fall only on the human being. It falls on the qarīn too. Both will be dragged into the Fire together.

This relationship between the human and their qarīn will be fully explained in Surah Qaf, where a conversation takes place between a person and their qarīn on the Day of Judgment. We will meet this personal enemy, this companion who stayed with us throughout our lives, and we will have words with them—because this is personal. إِنَّهُمْ كَانُوا۟ خَـٰسِرِينَ—“Indeed, they were losers.” They have been bankrupt, completely destroyed.

Ayah 26

وَقَالَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ لَا تَسْمَعُوا۟ لِهَـٰذَا ٱلْقُرْءَانِ وَٱلْغَوْا۟ فِيهِ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَغْلِبُونَ

The disbelievers developed a strategy: “Don’t listen to this Quran.” They couldn’t defeat it through debate, so they chose a different approach—distraction. وَٱلْغَوْا۟ فِيهِ means to make noise, to talk about something else, to bring up tangents. When the Quran is being discussed, change the subject. Talk about philosophy, bring up abstract concepts, discuss anything except the message at hand.

This tactic manifests in various ways. In the time of the Prophet ﷺ, there were those who hired singers from Syria. When the Quran was being recited, they would start their musical performances, drowning out the divine words. Today, this happens in every living room across the Muslim world. When someone wants to discuss the Quran, someone else turns up the music, suggests watching a movie, or insists on talking about something else. “This isn’t the right time. Let’s discuss this later.”

Why this resistance? Because when people start listening to the Quran, they change. And those who resist change have often seen others who were just like them transform completely after engaging with Allah’s ﷻ words. They became different, “weird” even. The fear of transformation, the desire to maintain the status quo, leads to this active avoidance. يُحِبُّونَ ٱلْعَمَى—they love their blindness and want to remain as they are.

The disbelievers understood something crucial: the only way to overpower this Quran is to prevent it from being heard. Debate failed. Intellectual challenge failed. So the final strategy became simple: ignore it, distract from it, make noise when it appears.

This disease has infected even Muslim discourse today. Where is the Quran in our conversations? When we discuss our families, our communities, our strategic direction as Muslims living in the West, where is the Quran in these discussions? It’s absent. We’ve relegated it to the masjid, to Quran competitions, to ultra-religious circles. We’ve created an assumption that the Quran belongs only to those who wear traditional Islamic dress—the kufi, the thobe, the turban, the hijab and niqab. When a “normal-looking” Muslim starts talking about the Quran, people are surprised. One person actually said, “Why are you talking about the Quran? You look normal.”

The secular academics and progressive thinkers, both in the Muslim world and here, operate under assumptions about the Quran without ever truly engaging with it. They assume it’s close-minded, that it restricts thinking, that it denies basic rights and dignities. They call for “rethinking” the Quran without ever having thought about it properly in the first place. They don’t know what it actually says; they only know their stereotypes about it.

This is why firsthand exposure to the Quran for every Muslim man and woman is absolutely critical. We have stereotypes—not just non-Muslims, but Muslims ourselves. Entertainment media, pseudo-academic discourse, and political spin, both here and in Muslim countries, work to undermine the Quran’s message. We must bring it back to the center of our lives and conversations.

Ayah 27

فَلَنُذِيقَنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ عَذَابًا شَدِيدًا وَلَنَجْزِيَنَّهُمْ أَسْوَأَ ٱلَّذِى كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ

Every mention of disbelievers in the Quran refers to a specific type, a specific flavor of kufr. Here, Allah ﷻ addresses those who campaign against the Quran being heard, those who actively create noise and distraction. For this particular brand of disbeliever, Allah ﷻ declares with emphatic oaths: فَلَنُذِيقَنَّ—“We will absolutely, certainly, definitely make them taste punishment.”

The construction of this promise is remarkable. The normal verb form is نُذِيق, but here we have four emphatic elements added to it—lām, nūn, and the intensive structure repeated. It’s as though Allah ﷻ is taking an oath three times over: We will make them taste, We will make them taste, We will make them taste intense, severe punishment.

But Allah ﷻ doesn’t stop there. وَلَنَجْزِيَنَّهُمْ أَسْوَأَ ٱلَّذِى كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ—“And We will compensate them with the worst of what they used to do.” Typically, Allah ﷻ promises to reward people with better than what they did. Here, the opposite: they will receive recompense for the worst of their actions.

Ayah 28

ذَٰلِكَ جَزَآءُ أَعْدَآءِ ٱللَّهِ ٱلنَّارُ ۖ لَهُمْ فِيهَا دَارُ ٱلْخُلْدِ ۖ جَزَآءًۢ بِمَا كَانُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا يَجْحَدُونَ

That is the payback, the recompense for the enemies of Allah ﷻ: the Fire. For them in it is the eternal home, the home of permanence. جَزَآءًۢ بِمَا كَانُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا يَجْحَدُونَ—a compensation in response to their denial, their resistance, their active opposition to Our signs.

Ayah 29

وَقَالَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ رَبَّنَآ أَرِنَا ٱلَّذَيْنِ أَضَلَّانَا مِنَ ٱلْجِنِّ وَٱلْإِنسِ نَجْعَلْهُمَا تَحْتَ أَقْدَامِنَا لِيَكُونَا مِنَ ٱلْأَسْفَلِينَ

On the Day of Judgment, these disbelievers will make a plea: “Our Master, show us the two who misguided us from among the jinn and mankind.” Show me my qarīn. Show me my jinn companion who fulfilled my every desire. Show me those human friends I used to listen to, whose advice I followed without question, who led me to this place. “Show them to us so we can step on them, so we can put them under our feet, so they can be among the lowest ones.”

The bitter irony: they seek revenge against those who misguided them, yet it was their own choice to follow that guidance.

Ayah 30

إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ قَالُوا۟ رَبُّنَا ٱللَّهُ ثُمَّ ٱسْتَقَـٰمُوا۟ تَتَنَزَّلُ عَلَيْهِمُ ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةُ أَلَّا تَخَافُوا۟ وَلَا تَحْزَنُوا۟ وَأَبْشِرُوا۟ بِٱلْجَنَّةِ ٱلَّتِى كُنتُمْ تُوعَدُونَ

Now we arrive at what may be the central passage of this entire Surah. إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ قَالُوا۟ رَبُّنَا ٱللَّهُ—“Indeed, those who said, ‘Our Lord is Allah…’” This is the easy part. Saying لَا إِلٰهَ إِلَّا ٱللَّٰهُ is simple. The tongue can utter it easily—كَلِمَةٌ خَفِيفَةٌ عَلَى ٱللِّسَانِ (a word light upon the tongue). You can say it while driving, while sitting, even in moments of distraction. You can make dhikr of these words constantly without much effort.

But then comes ثُمَّ ٱسْتَقَـٰمُوا—“then they remained firm.” They stood up for it. They tried to stay standing. This is the image of someone who was lying down asleep, then woke up and stood. The one who just woke doesn’t want to go back to sleep now. They want to remain awake, to stay standing despite the storm.

Standing in the middle of a storm requires something to hold onto. You need an anchor that doesn’t budge—the Quran itself. When your family hates your newfound commitment, when society rejects you, when friends abandon you, when your own internal urges—desires you’ve fulfilled your entire life—suddenly must be controlled, that’s when istiqamah is tested.

There’s a brother from Richardson, Texas—a white professional convert—whose story captures this struggle. His friends gather every weekend for drinks at bars. They go skiing together, watch movies, engage in free mixing between men and women. This was his entire social life. Then, out of nowhere, he became Muslim. Now he sits with Muslim brothers for dinner, and the weight of his new reality hits him: “All those gatherings, everything I’ve done my whole life—I can’t do that anymore. Everyone’s noticing I’m acting strange. I haven’t even told them I’m Muslim yet. I don’t know how long I can hold on.”

This is the split: قَالُوا۟ رَبُّنَا ٱللَّهُ is Part One. Then comes ثُمَّ ٱسْتَقَـٰمُوا—Part Two, where you try to stay standing, to remain firm, to weather the storm.

Earlier in this Surah, Allah ﷻ commanded: ٱسْتَقِيمُوا۟ إِلَيْهِ—“Stand firm for Him” (41:6). Now, in the thick of the Surah, He tells us about those who actually did it—and what He does for them.

There’s another connection. In ayah 14, Allah ﷻ mentioned that when messengers came to the disbelievers from every direction, calling them to worship Allah ﷻ alone, the disbelievers said, “If our Lord had willed, He would have sent down angels.” They demanded angels instead of human messengers.

Now Allah ﷻ reveals: He does send angels—but not for the disbelievers. He sends them for the believers, for those who stood their ground. تَتَنَزَّلُ عَلَيْهِمُ ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةُ—“The angels descend upon them.”

According to the near-consensus of mufassirūn, this descent occurs at a specific, crucial moment: at the time of death. This person held on throughout their life. They struggled, they fought, they remained standing until they literally couldn’t stand anymore. Now they’re on their deathbed, unable to utter words, their energy depleted. Family surrounds them, crying, about to witness their departure from this world.

In this moment, in his final breaths, he sees something no one else in that hospital room can see. He sees angels—not just one angel, but legions of angels descending upon him. And while he’s still alive, still in this world, they speak to him: أَلَّا تَخَافُوا۟—“Don’t be afraid.” You’re about to die, and fear is natural, but don’t be afraid.

وَلَا تَحْزَنُوا۟—“And don’t be sad.” Why would he be sad? He’s leaving his family behind, his loved ones. The angels acknowledge this natural grief and tell him: Don’t be sad.

وَأَبْشِرُوا۟ بِٱلْجَنَّةِ ٱلَّتِى كُنتُمْ تُوعَدُونَ—“Receive good news of Jannah, which you were promised.” Remember that Jannah you worked toward all this time? That one you struggled for when you stood your ground against temptation? You’re getting it. Now. This is yours.

Imagine the scene: this person doesn’t even have the muscle strength left to smile. If only his family could see what he sees. If only they could know. Everyone around him weeps while he experiences the happiest moment of his entire existence. The profound irony of it—his family sees ending, while he sees beginning.

Ayah 31

نَحْنُ أَوْلِيَآؤُكُمْ فِى ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا وَفِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ ۖ وَلَكُمْ فِيهَا مَا تَشْتَهِىٓ أَنفُسُكُمْ وَلَكُمْ فِيهَا مَا تَدَّعُونَ

The angels continue: “We are your protective friends—your awliya’—in the worldly life and in the Hereafter.” Remember when you decided to stand up for Allah ﷻ, and you felt the storm threatening to knock you down? We were there. When Shaytan came to pull you away from your commitment, we battled those devils for you. We were your support system, your security detail, your protective friends throughout this worldly life.

No believer should ever feel alone. Even the only Muslim in an entire town, the only practicing believer in their family, the single person standing firm among thousands—they have an army of angels surrounding them. نَحْنُ أَوْلِيَآؤُكُمْ. There are students who come from islands with only fifty Muslims total, sent abroad to learn and bring knowledge back. Even they aren’t alone. Angels protect them.

وَفِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ—“And in the Hereafter” too. When the Day of Judgment arrives and everything falls apart, when chaos surrounds you, you still have your guardian angels, your security detail, standing with you.

وَلَكُمْ فِيهَا مَا تَشْتَهِىٓ أَنفُسُكُمْ—“And you will have therein whatever your souls desire.” This addresses a crucial truth: remaining firm on Islam doesn’t mean your desires died. You still had temptations. You still wanted to look there, to try that, to take advantage of opportunities that came your way. The thought crossed your mind: “I wish I could do that.” But you stopped yourself. You fought those temptations with الأَعُوذُ بِاللّٰهِ مِنَ الشَّيْطَانِ الرَّجِيمِ.

Now the gates of fulfillment open. مَا تَشْتَهِىٓ أَنفُسُكُمْ—whatever you desired, you have it. Every shahwah (temptation), every strong desire you fought against in this world, can now be fulfilled without shame, without sin, without consequences.

وَلَكُمْ فِيهَا مَا تَدَّعُونَ—“And you will have whatever you request.” Shahwah represents what all humans naturally desire—what Allah ﷻ built into our nature. But da’wā is different. It represents what we ask for at different levels of maturity.

Ask a young child what they want most in the world, and they’ll say ice cream. Only ice cream. Ask an older child, and they’ll request an iPad, then a MacBook, then a TV in their room, then WiFi access. Our requests change as we mature and our understanding expands.

In Jannah, you have both: the fulfillment of every natural desire you suppressed for Allah’s ﷻ sake, and the granting of every request your heart makes—no matter your level, no matter your maturity, no matter what stage of eternal joy you’ve reached. You name it, you have it.

This is what awaited those who simply said “Our Lord is Allah” and then tried—truly tried—to remain standing.

Ayah 31–33 · linked video: 04. Fussilat (Ayah 31-33)

Surah Fussilat: Ayahs 31-33

Ayah 31

نَحْنُ أَوْلِيَآؤُكُمْ فِى ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا وَفِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ ۖ وَلَكُمْ فِيهَا مَا تَشْتَهِىٓ أَنفُسُكُمْ وَلَكُمْ فِيهَا مَا تَدَّعُونَ

We are your protectors in the life of this world and in the Hereafter. And you will have therein whatever your souls desire, and you will have therein whatever you request.

The angels continue their conversation with the believers, extending their promise of companionship beyond this world. They declare themselves as awliya (protectors, guardians, allies) in both realms—a profound comfort for those who have maintained their faith despite opposition.

The promise “you will have therein whatever your souls desire” speaks to something universal about human nature. Our wants and desires change based on our life experiences and stages. What a 40-year-old desires differs from what a 30-year-old wants. Someone who has lived their entire life in a village will have different aspirations than someone who grew up in a city. People are at different levels with different life experiences, so their wants at some level are the same, but at another level they are completely unique.

Allah ﷻ addresses both dimensions. The phrase “whatever your souls desire” encompasses those common human longings—the universal temptations and aspirations we all share. But then He ﷻ adds something remarkable: وَلَكُمْ فِيهَا مَا تَدَّعُونَ—“and you will have therein whatever you request.” This speaks to your personal order, your unique curiosity, the questions that only you have asked. Nobody else wondered about that particular thing—just you. And you’ll receive that answer too.

Consider the person whose deepest desire in life was to understand the Book of Allah ﷻ. Perhaps they spent years wondering about the meaning of certain passages, puzzling over the mysteries of creation. They might finally ask in Paradise, “Can You tell me what the seven heavens really are? What does the second heaven do? What about the third? Can I finally know?” And they will receive that knowledge as a gift.

This is deeply personal. Perhaps someone wants to meet the first person in their ancestry who accepted Islam. Imagine the powerful story of faith preserved through generations—the struggles, the sacrifices, the determination to pass down the religion despite overwhelming odds. That curiosity, that longing to know one’s spiritual heritage, will be fulfilled.

The reality of how our faith reaches us is itself miraculous. For many of us, Islam came through our parents, but it wasn’t genetic—it came at a price. Stories of migration, of families torn apart and put back together, of survival against impossible odds—these are the threads that connect us to our faith. The desire to understand that chain, to honor those who preserved it, is a longing that Paradise will satisfy.

Ayah 32

نُزُلًا مِّنْ غَفُورٍ رَّحِيمٍ

[All this is] accommodation from the Exceedingly Forgiving, the Always Merciful.

The word نُزُل is significant here—it refers to the hospitality offered to a guest when they first arrive, the introductory refreshment served while the main meal is being prepared. In Arab culture, this is the chai, the light snack meant to wet your appetite before the real feast begins.

Allah ﷻ is telling us that everything described so far—having whatever your soul desires, whatever you request—this is just the nuzul. This is just the appetizer. Your appetite will be satisfied by something far greater than even the biggest thing you can imagine. What Paradise truly has to offer cannot fit within the limits of our imagination.

As the hadith tells us, there are things in Paradise that no eye has seen, no ear has heard, that haven’t even entered human imagination. How can you ask for something you’ve never seen? How can you desire something you haven’t heard of? How can you request something that hasn’t even entered your thoughts? This ayah establishes that the wonders we’ve been promised are merely a starting point.

And this magnificent gift comes مِّنْ غَفُورٍ رَّحِيمٍ—from the One who is Exceedingly Forgiving, Always Merciful. The choice of these two names is deliberate. Despite our shortcomings and sins, His ﷻ forgiveness opens the door to this hospitality. And His ﷻ endless mercy ensures that what He gives exceeds anything we could have earned or deserved.

Ayah 33

وَمَنْ أَحْسَنُ قَوْلًا مِّمَّن دَعَآ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ وَعَمِلَ صَـٰلِحًا وَقَالَ إِنَّنِى مِنَ ٱلْمُسْلِمِينَ

And who is better in speech than one who calls to Allah, acts righteously, and says, “Indeed, I am among the Muslims”?

This ayah shifts focus to the role of the believer in this world. Allah ﷻ asks a rhetorical question: Who could be better in terms of what they say, and who could be a better person, than someone who calls people to Allah ﷻ? The Arabic works linguistically in both directions—it’s a compliment to both the speech and the speaker.

But notice the conditions attached to this noble calling. It’s not just about talking; it’s about وَعَمِلَ صَـٰلِحًا—acting righteously. The previous ayahs established the importance of holding onto the religion for yourself, weathering the storm of opposition and difficulty. Now it’s time to not only maintain your own faith but to help others who are drowning in the storm, to help them stand up as well.

The connection between calling to Allah ﷻ and righteous action is inseparable. You can understand this phrase in multiple ways. He calls to Allah ﷻ while acting righteously—meaning if he wasn’t acting righteously, his call to Allah ﷻ would be empty words with no weight. But you can also understand it as: he calls to Allah ﷻ by acting righteously. His righteous deeds themselves become the call to Allah ﷻ. His actions speak louder than his words, drawing people to the religion through the beauty of Islamic character.

Then comes the remarkable conclusion: وَقَالَ إِنَّنِى مِنَ ٱلْمُسْلِمِينَ—“and he says, ‘Indeed, I am among the Muslims.’” This statement is extraordinary because it simultaneously expresses both pride and humility—a rare combination in the Quran.

From one perspective, it’s a statement of pride: I am proud to be Muslim. I don’t hide it; I declare it openly. During the time of revelation, when the Quraysh were persecuting and mocking the Muslims, standing up and declaring oneself a Muslim took courage. And who could have more honor than the one who gets to call people to Allah ﷻ? What a tremendous privilege that is.

But from another angle, this same statement expresses profound humility: I am just a Muslim, like you. I’m no one special. The person calling to Allah ﷻ must help people understand that he doesn’t speak from a pedestal above them, that he isn’t claiming to be holier than they are. Just because someone stands to give a lecture, just because microphones are clipped to their clothes and recordings are made, doesn’t mean they’re in a better position than those listening. They have angels on their shoulders just like everyone else. They have a guardian just like everyone else. They face struggles just like everyone else.

The one quoting the ayahs is not necessarily closer to guidance than the one listening to them. We’re all in the same boat. We’re all Muslims working toward Allah’s ﷻ pleasure.

Notice that the ayah doesn’t say “I am from the true believers” or “I am from the righteous.” It says مِنَ ٱلْمُسْلِمِينَ—from among the Muslims. Muslim is the starting point, isn’t it? Then comes mu’min (believer), then muhsin (one who perfects their worship). But the caller to Allah ﷻ identifies with the entry level: I’m just Muslim. I’m not claiming superiority just because I’m calling people to Allah ﷻ.

This teaching is critical for our community. We cannot place people in positions of religious authority on unnatural pedestals. It’s spiritually poisonous. When we elevate scholars, imams, speakers and teachers beyond their humanity, we create unhealthy expectations—angelic standards we don’t apply to ourselves. We expect perfection, and then when we spend time with them and realize they’re human (perhaps more obviously human than ourselves), we swing to the opposite extreme: “I’ll never listen to him again. He’s just a regular person. I saw him sleeping once!”

We seem unable to keep things normal. Either someone is way up on a pedestal or they’ve fallen completely. This is not the Islamic approach.

The caller to Allah ﷻ simply says: إِنَّنِى مِنَ ٱلْمُسْلِمِينَ—I am among the Muslims. That’s it. Keep it normal.

However, there’s an important balance here. While the caller to Allah ﷻ must maintain humility and not claim superiority, they must also hold themselves to a high standard. The reality is that most people are not mature enough to separate the messenger from the message. They will see you as the representation of your message. Any flaw of yours will be perceived as a flaw of the message itself.

This is why anyone in a position of responsibility must be vigilant about self-improvement. And all of us occupy positions of responsibility. If you’re the head of a household, you’re in a position of responsibility. You’re already a role model. Children look up to you. Your spouse looks up to you—yes, even if it’s not expressed openly. Your extended family looks up to you. Community members look up to you.

When people look up to you, you must hold yourself to a tougher standard. You must constantly be aware of what you can change about yourself for the better. This is the reality captured in this powerful ayah: Who could be better in speech than one who calls to Allah ﷻ, acts righteously, and declares with both pride and humility, “I am among the Muslims”?

Ayah 34–47 · linked video: 05. Fussilat (Ayah 34-47)

Surah Fussilat: Ayahs 34-47

Ayah 34

وَلَا تَسْتَوِى ٱلْحَسَنَةُ وَلَا ٱلسَّيِّئَةُ ۚ ٱدْفَعْ بِٱلَّتِى هِىَ أَحْسَنُ فَإِذَا ٱلَّذِى بَيْنَكَ وَبَيْنَهُۥ عَدَٰوَةٌ كَأَنَّهُۥ وَلِىٌّ حَمِيمٌ

The Quran is remarkably concise in its language, and this ayah exemplifies that quality. Allah ﷻ declares: the good and the evil are not the same. While the wording is brief, the context reveals its full meaning. Good deeds and bad deeds are fundamentally different, but this ayah addresses something more specific—the conduct of those calling people to Allah ﷻ.

When you engage in dawah, when you speak to people about this deen and about the Quran, you will inevitably encounter offensive responses. People may say rude and inappropriate things to you. This ayah establishes a crucial principle: your response must be good, not bad, because these two reactions do not carry equal weight before Allah ﷻ.

The command إِدْفَعْ بِٱلَّتِى هِىَ أَحْسَنُ means “repel with that which is better.” When someone says something harmful to you, you must respond with something better. Turn a bad situation into an opportunity for good. Look at the example of Musa (AS)—when people said terrible things to him, he turned those moments into opportunities and responded only with goodness.

Then Allah ﷻ describes the remarkable result of this approach: فَإِذَا ٱلَّذِى بَيْنَكَ وَبَيْنَهُۥ عَدَٰوَةٌ كَأَنَّهُۥ وَلِىٌّ حَمِيمٌ—“so behold, the one between whom and you there was enmity, it is as though he is a close, devoted friend.”

This ayah teaches several profound lessons. First, at the level of dawah, you must speak to people as though you are their most intimate friend and they are yours. You must be patient, roll with the punches, and maintain an extremely friendly attitude. Notice that Allah ﷻ says كَأَنَّهُۥ—“as though he is.” It’s not that the person actually is your friend at that moment, but your attitude and behavior must reflect that they are. You cannot display anger. You cannot respond poorly when they speak poorly. You must treat them as the friendliest person to you.

Second, the word كَأَنَّهُۥ implies hope. Even though right now there is عَدَٰوَة (animosity) between you, there may come a day when that very person embraces Islam. What began as hostility might end in the closest friendship, with them protecting you as a وَلِىٌّ حَمِيمٌ—a protective, intimate companion.

Ayah 35

وَمَا يُلَقَّىٰهَآ إِلَّا ٱلَّذِينَ صَبَرُوا۟ وَمَا يُلَقَّىٰهَآ إِلَّا ذُو حَظٍّ عَظِيمٍ

This standard of conduct is extraordinarily difficult to maintain. Allah ﷻ acknowledges this reality by telling us who can truly meet it: وَمَا يُلَقَّىٰهَآ إِلَّا ٱلَّذِينَ صَبَرُوا۟—“And none will be granted this except those who are patient.” The word يُلَقَّىٰهَآ means to meet or reach a standard. Only those who possess deep, unwavering patience will be able to maintain this level of character when faced with hostility and insult.

Earlier in the surah, we learned that the first challenge for someone speaking publicly about Islam is humility—إِنَّنِى مِنَ ٱلْمُسْلِمِينَ. The next great challenge, once humility is established, is patience. If humility is absent, patience cannot exist either. When someone questions you harshly, heckles you, or responds badly, you will become impatient with them. You’ll think, “I’m speaking to you, doing you a favor—how dare they talk back to me?” So this ayah emphasizes that only people of ultimate, perfected patience will reach this level.

Then Allah ﷻ provides powerful motivation: وَمَا يُلَقَّىٰهَآ إِلَّا ذُو حَظٍّ عَظِيمٍ—“And none will be granted this except one who possesses a great fortune.” The phrase حَظٍّ عَظِيمٍ refers to tremendous good luck, great blessing, immense fortune.

The last time we encountered this phrase was in the story of Qarun, when people looked at his massive wealth and said, “He has such حَظٍّ عَظِيمٍ—such great fortune.” Now Allah ﷻ is redefining what true fortune means. The real حَظٍّ عَظِيمٍ belongs to the person who has given up worldly concerns, who calls people to Allah ﷻ, who absorbs insults, and who responds with patience. That is the real treasure. That is the truly fortunate one.

When your patience is being tested in dawah, remind yourself of how blessed you are to be given this role, this opportunity to call people to Allah ﷻ.

Ayah 36

وَإِمَّا يَنزَغَنَّكَ مِنَ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنِ نَزْغٌ فَٱسْتَعِذْ بِٱللَّهِ ۖ إِنَّهُۥ هُوَ ٱلسَّمِيعُ ٱلْعَلِيمُ

Even though you are patient, you are still human. Shaytan will exploit this reality. The word نَزْغٌ means a poke or provocation—something that disturbs you, that tries to get under your skin. Shaytan whispers: “Did you hear what they just said to you? How can you let them get away with that?”

Sometimes Shaytan’s prodding won’t get through immediately. But if the person keeps attacking and attacking, saying hurtful things, eventually their words might pierce through your patience. Even though you’ve been maintaining sabr, even though you’ve been treating them like a friend, at the end of the day their relentless attacks might finally hurt you. Your anger might take hold of you—and this moment of lost temper is what Allah ﷻ describes as Shaytan’s نَزْغٌ.

The remedy is immediate: فَٱسْتَعِذْ بِٱللَّهِ—“seek refuge in Allah.” The moment you realize that your anger and loss of control is the work of Shaytan, immediately turn to Allah ﷻ for protection. Say: أَعُوذُ بِٱللَّهِ مِنَ ٱلشَّيْطَانِ ٱلرَّجِيمِ.

إِنَّهُۥ هُوَ ٱلسَّمِيعُ ٱلْعَلِيمُ—“Indeed, He is the All-Hearing, the All-Knowing.” He hears everything that was said to you. He knows everything you’re feeling and struggling with. This knowledge should bring you comfort and strength.

Ayah 37

وَمِنْ ءَايَـٰتِهِ ٱلَّيْلُ وَٱلنَّهَارُ وَٱلشَّمْسُ وَٱلْقَمَرُ ۚ لَا تَسْجُدُوا۟ لِلشَّمْسِ وَلَا لِلْقَمَرِ وَٱسْجُدُوا۟ لِلَّهِ ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَهُنَّ إِن كُنتُمْ إِيَّاهُ تَعْبُدُونَ

Having addressed the ethics and character of the one who calls to Allah ﷻ, the passage now turns to the content of the message itself. وَمِنْ ءَايَـٰتِهِ ٱلَّيْلُ وَٱلنَّهَارُ وَٱلشَّمْسُ وَٱلْقَمَرُ—“And among His signs are the night and the day, the sun and the moon.”

Then comes the command: لَا تَسْجُدُوا۟ لِلشَّمْسِ وَلَا لِلْقَمَرِ—“Do not prostrate to the sun, nor to the moon.” This phrasing should remind us of someone who went through a personal journey of rejecting sun and moon worship: Ibrahim (AS). He reflected on the sun and moon, considering whether they could be worthy of worship, and ultimately concluded that they were not—only their Creator deserved his prostration.

By using this specific wording, Allah ﷻ connects us to that story. Remember your father Ibrahim (AS) and his journey to pure monotheism. وَٱسْجُدُوا۟ لِلَّهِ ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَهُنَّ—“And prostrate to Allah, who created them all.” إِن كُنتُمْ إِيَّاهُ تَعْبُدُونَ—“If indeed it is Him alone that you worship.”

Ayah 38

فَإِنِ ٱسْتَكْبَرُوا۟ فَٱلَّذِينَ عِندَ رَبِّكَ يُسَبِّحُونَ لَهُۥ بِٱلَّيْلِ وَٱلنَّهَارِ وَهُمْ لَا يَسْـَٔمُونَ ۩

فَإِنِ ٱسْتَكْبَرُوا۟—“But if they show arrogance” and refuse to worship Allah ﷻ alone, then let them know: فَٱلَّذِينَ عِندَ رَبِّكَ يُسَبِّحُونَ لَهُۥ بِٱلَّيْلِ وَٱلنَّهَارِ—“Those who are in the company of your Lord glorify Him night and day.”

This refers primarily to the angels, but because they haven’t been explicitly named, some scholars say it also includes the most elite worshippers among Allah’s slaves on earth—those who have reached the highest levels of devotion.

وَهُمْ لَا يَسْـَٔمُونَ—“And they never grow weary.” The word يَسْـَٔمُونَ comes from سَأَمَ, which means to be fed up and irritated with something, to lose motivation and become annoyed. It’s more than simple laziness (كَسَل)—it’s the motivations behind laziness. When someone drags their feet doing something, it’s because they’re fed up with it, it irritates them, and they don’t want to do it anymore. That combination of feelings is سَأَمَ.

For example, in the context of writing loan agreements, Allah ﷻ says وَلَا تَسْـَٔمُوٓا۟ أَن تَكْتُبُوهُ—“And do not be fed up with writing it down” (2:282). Imagine being the only literate person in your village—everyone comes to you to document their transactions. One after another after another. You’d naturally get fed up and irritated. Allah ﷻ commands: don’t drag your feet, don’t get lazy about this religious obligation.

Similarly here, the angels and righteous servants never show such laziness or weariness in glorifying Allah ﷻ. Perhaps this includes us as well—may Allah ﷻ make us among those who maintain constant glorification of Him. This is the place of sajdah in the Quran, where we join in that glorification.

Ayah 39

وَمِنْ ءَايَـٰتِهِۦٓ أَنَّكَ تَرَى ٱلْأَرْضَ خَـٰشِعَةً فَإِذَآ أَنزَلْنَا عَلَيْهَا ٱلْمَآءَ ٱهْتَزَّتْ وَرَبَتْ ۚ إِنَّ ٱلَّذِىٓ أَحْيَاهَا لَمُحْىِ ٱلْمَوْتَىٰٓ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ

Another miraculous sign: وَمِنْ ءَايَـٰتِهِۦٓ أَنَّكَ تَرَى ٱلْأَرْضَ خَـٰشِعَةً—“And among His signs is that you see the earth humble, still, lifeless.” The word خَـٰشِعَةً describes the earth in a state of complete stillness and humility, dry and barren.

فَإِذَآ أَنزَلْنَا عَلَيْهَا ٱلْمَآءَ ٱهْتَزَّتْ وَرَبَتْ—“Then when We send down water upon it, it stirs and swells.” The moment rain touches that dead earth, it trembles with life and rises up with growth.

إِنَّ ٱلَّذِىٓ أَحْيَاهَا لَمُحْىِ ٱلْمَوْتَىٰٓ—“Indeed, the One who brings it to life will surely bring the dead back to life.” This is a powerful proof of resurrection. The One who revives dead earth with water can certainly revive dead human beings.

إِنَّهُۥ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ—“Indeed, He has complete power over all things.”

Ayah 40

إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ يُلْحِدُونَ فِىٓ ءَايَـٰتِنَا لَا يَخْفَوْنَ عَلَيْنَآ ۗ أَفَمَن يُلْقَىٰ فِى ٱلنَّارِ خَيْرٌ أَم مَّن يَأْتِىٓ ءَامِنًا يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ ۚ ٱعْمَلُوا۟ مَا شِئْتُمْ ۖ إِنَّهُۥ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ بَصِيرٌ

The word يُلْحِدُونَ comes from إِلْحَاد, which originally refers to making something crooked. When digging a grave, it should be a straight rectangle—if you make it crooked, that’s called لَحْد. The word also refers to being biased toward or against something, deviating from what is straight.

إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ يُلْحِدُونَ فِىٓ ءَايَـٰتِنَا—“Indeed, those who deviate regarding Our signs, who create distortion in Our ayat.” لَا يَخْفَوْنَ عَلَيْنَآ—“They are not hidden from Us.” Nothing they do escapes Allah’s knowledge.

Then comes a rhetorical question: أَفَمَن يُلْقَىٰ فِى ٱلنَّارِ خَيْرٌ أَم مَّن يَأْتِىٓ ءَامِنًا يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ—“Is the one who is thrown into the Fire better, or the one who comes secure on the Day of Resurrection?” The word ءَامِنًا means safely, securely, with faith and without fear.

ٱعْمَلُوا۟ مَا شِئْتُمْ—“Do whatever you wish.” This phrase echoes the challenge the disbelievers issued to the Messenger ﷺ earlier in the surah. They said, “Our hearts are sealed, our ears are blocked, there’s a barrier between us and you—so go ahead and do whatever you’re going to do.” Now Allah ﷻ turns that challenge back on them: Do whatever you want.

But this is not permission—it’s a warning. إِنَّهُۥ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ بَصِيرٌ—“Indeed, He has full view of whatever you do.” Earlier we learned that their movements and activities should not bother the Prophet ﷺ, with the implication that even their hidden schemes are not hidden from Allah ﷻ. Now that point is made explicit: He sees everything.

Ayah 41

إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ بِٱلذِّكْرِ لَمَّا جَآءَهُمْ ۖ وَإِنَّهُۥ لَكِتَـٰبٌ عَزِيزٌ

إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ بِٱلذِّكْرِ لَمَّا جَآءَهُمْ—“Indeed, those who disbelieved in the Reminder when it came to them…”

Before continuing, we should understand why the Quran is called الذِّكْرِ—the Reminder. A reminder is never something new. New things in Arabic are called حَدِيث. But one of the names of the Quran is ذِكْر, which means reminder, and reminders are never new—they refer to something you already knew before.

This name fortifies a fundamental principle in our religion: we already know about Allah ﷻ. We already know about guidance. We already know about qadr. We already know about our role in life. This knowledge was embedded in us from the beginning—when our souls testified before Allah ﷻ, “Am I not your Lord?” and we said, “Yes!” The Quran comes to remind us of that original covenant, to awaken what’s already inside us.

The ayah begins describing those who rejected this Reminder when it came to them, but then doesn’t complete the sentence with their punishment. Instead, Allah ﷻ shifts focus: وَإِنَّهُۥ لَكِتَـٰبٌ عَزِيزٌ—“And indeed, it is a Book of authority and power.” The consequences for rejecting it are so heavy, so overwhelming, that they’re not even mentioned. All Allah ﷻ says is: know that this is a mighty, authoritative, powerful book. You’re dealing with something immensely powerful.

Ayah 42

لَّا يَأْتِيهِ ٱلْبَـٰطِلُ مِنۢ بَيْنِ يَدَيْهِ وَلَا مِنْ خَلْفِهِۦ ۖ تَنزِيلٌ مِّنْ حَكِيمٍ حَمِيدٍ

This ayah describes the Quran’s invincibility: لَّا يَأْتِيهِ ٱلْبَـٰطِلُ مِنۢ بَيْنِ يَدَيْهِ وَلَا مِنْ خَلْفِهِۦ—“Falsehood cannot approach it from before it or from behind it.” The Quran cannot be attacked. You cannot insult it. You cannot weaken it. It is عَزِيزٌ—powerful and unassailable.

تَنزِيلٌ مِّنْ حَكِيمٍ حَمِيدٍ—“A revelation from the All-Wise, the Praiseworthy.” It comes from One who is حَكِيمٍ, so its wisdom cannot be questioned or toppled. And it comes from One who is حَمِيدٍ, already possessing all praise in and of Himself, so it cannot be cursed or put down.

Muslims don’t need to worry about defending the Quran. The Quran defends us. It was sent with its own عِزَّة—its own authority and power. We don’t need to give it strength; it already possesses that inherently.

Ayah 43

مَّا يُقَالُ لَكَ إِلَّا مَا قَدْ قِيلَ لِلرُّسُلِ مِن قَبْلِكَ ۚ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ لَذُو مَغْفِرَةٍ وَذُو عِقَابٍ أَلِيمٍ

مَّا يُقَالُ لَكَ إِلَّا مَا قَدْ قِيلَ لِلرُّسُلِ مِن قَبْلِكَ—“Nothing is being said to you except what has already been said to messengers before you.” This is comfort for the Prophet ﷺ. The insults, the rejections, the challenges he faces—none of this is new. Every messenger before him heard similar things from their people.

إِنَّ رَبَّكَ لَذُو مَغْفِرَةٍ وَذُو عِقَابٍ أَلِيمٍ—“Indeed, your Lord possesses great forgiveness, and He also possesses painful punishment.” This dual nature of Allah’s attributes—His mercy and His justice—runs throughout the Quran. He is merciful to those who turn to Him, severe with those who persist in rebellion.

Ayah 44

وَلَوْ جَعَلْنَـٰهُ قُرْءَانًا أَعْجَمِيًّا لَّقَالُوا۟ لَوْلَا فُصِّلَتْ ءَايَـٰتُهُۥٓ ۖ ءَا۬عْجَمِىٌّ وَعَرَبِىٌّ ۗ قُلْ هُوَ لِلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ هُدًى وَشِفَآءٌ ۖ وَٱلَّذِينَ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ فِىٓ ءَاذَانِهِمْ وَقْرٌ وَهُوَ عَلَيْهِمْ عَمًى ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ يُنَادَوْنَ مِن مَّكَانٍۭ بَعِيدٍ

Some objectors asked: Why is this Quran in Arabic? If it’s revelation from Allah ﷻ, who is above the heavens, shouldn’t it be in some other language? Allah ﷻ responds: وَلَوْ جَعَلْنَـٰهُ قُرْءَانًا أَعْجَمِيًّا—“And if We had made it a Quran in a foreign language…”

لَّقَالُوا۟ لَوْلَا فُصِّلَتْ ءَايَـٰتُهُۥٓ—“They would have said: ‘Why are its verses not made clear?’” They would have complained: We don’t understand any of it. The ayat aren’t clear to us. We don’t know where one sentence ends and another begins because it’s not our language.

ءَا۬عْجَمِىٌّ وَعَرَبِىٌّ—“A foreign tongue and an Arab audience?” How does that make sense? The messenger is Arab, the audience is Arab—how could the message be in a foreign language?

قُلْ هُوَ لِلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ هُدًى وَشِفَآءٌ—“Say: It is, for those who believe, guidance and healing.” The Quran serves two profound functions for believers: it guides them to the straight path, and it heals them.

The mention of شِفَآءٌ (healing) in connection with the Arabic Quran is significant. We’re learning that the healing power of the Quran is tied to its Arabic nature. This connects to the practice of the Prophet ﷺ, who would perform ruqyah—reciting Quran over people for spiritual and physical healing.

In a spiritual sense, the Quran cures diseases of the heart: jealousy, hatred, depression, sadness, anxiety, cowardice. It also provides healing for those afflicted by jinn, the evil eye, or magic—conditions that prevent sleep or cause other disturbances. And it offers healing for physical ailments as well. When you visit someone in the hospital, you can perform ruqyah on them. This is the Prophetic practice, and the Quran itself declares that it is شِفَآءٌ.

وَٱلَّذِينَ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ فِىٓ ءَاذَانِهِمْ وَقْرٌ—“And those who do not believe—in their ears is deafness.” They have barriers in their ears; they cannot truly hear the message. وَهُوَ عَلَيْهِمْ عَمًى—“And it is blindness upon them.” Not only can they not hear it, they cannot see its truth.

أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ يُنَادَوْنَ مِن مَّكَانٍۭ بَعِيدٍ—“Those are being called from a distant place.” Imagine standing far, far away from someone who’s speaking to you. You can barely see them and you can’t make out what they’re saying—that’s their relationship with the Quran’s message. The imagery combines both their deafness and blindness.

Another meaning: they themselves have gone to a place far, far away. They’re not just slightly misguided—they’ve gone way off the deep end, and it’s very unlikely they’ll come back from there.

Ayah 45

وَلَقَدْ ءَاتَيْنَا مُوسَى ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ فَٱخْتُلِفَ فِيهِ ۗ وَلَوْلَا كَلِمَةٌ سَبَقَتْ مِن رَّبِّكَ لَقُضِىَ بَيْنَهُمْ ۚ وَإِنَّهُمْ لَفِى شَكٍّ مِّنْهُ مُرِيبٍ

وَلَقَدْ ءَاتَيْنَا مُوسَى ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ فَٱخْتُلِفَ فِيهِ—“And We had already given Musa (AS) the Scripture, and it was disagreed about.” The Children of Israel disputed about the Torah just as the Quraysh dispute about the Quran. Disagreement about divine revelation is nothing new.
