وَلَوْلَا كَلِمَةٌ سَبَقَتْ مِن رَّبِّكَ لَقُضِىَ بَيْنَهُمْ—“And had not a word already preceded from your Lord, their matter would have been decided between them.” Allah ﷻ has decreed that judgment will be delayed until an appointed time. If not for this prior decree, He would have settled their affair immediately.

وَإِنَّهُمْ لَفِى شَكٍّ مِّنْهُ مُرِيبٍ—“And indeed, they are in disturbing doubt about it.” The word مُرِيبٍ describes a doubt that is infectious, that spreads to others and creates disturbance. They’re not just in doubt themselves—their doubt is of the kind that puts others in doubt as well, that grows and multiplies.

Ayah 46

مَّنْ عَمِلَ صَـٰلِحًا فَلِنَفْسِهِۦ ۖ وَمَنْ أَسَآءَ فَعَلَيْهَا ۗ وَمَا رَبُّكَ بِظَلَّـٰمٍ لِّلْعَبِيدِ

This ayah establishes perfect divine justice: مَّنْ عَمِلَ صَـٰلِحًا فَلِنَفْسِهِۦ—“Whoever does righteousness, it is for his own soul.” Notice it says عَمِلَ (did action) but then صَـٰلِحًا (while righteous)—so the emphasis is on doing actions while in a state of righteousness, while being صالح.

The benefit of righteous deeds returns entirely to the one who performed them. وَمَنْ أَسَآءَ فَعَلَيْهَا—“And whoever does evil, it is against it.” The harm of evil deeds falls entirely on the one who committed them.

وَمَا رَبُّكَ بِظَلَّـٰمٍ لِّلْعَبِيدِ—“And your Lord is not at all unjust to His servants.” The phrase بِظَلَّـٰمٍ uses the intensive form—He is not a tyrant at all, not even remotely unjust. Every reward is earned, every punishment is deserved. Perfect justice will be rendered.

Ayah 47

۞ إِلَيْهِ يُرَدُّ عِلْمُ ٱلسَّاعَةِ ۚ وَمَا تَخْرُجُ مِن ثَمَرَٰتٍ مِّنْ أَكْمَامِهَا وَمَا تَحْمِلُ مِنْ أُنثَىٰ وَلَا تَضَعُ إِلَّا بِعِلْمِهِۦ ۚ وَيَوْمَ يُنَادِيهِمْ أَيْنَ شُرَكَآءِى قَالُوٓا۟ ءَاذَنَّـٰكَ مَا مِنَّا مِن شَهِيدٍ

إِلَيْهِ يُرَدُّ عِلْمُ ٱلسَّاعَةِ—“To Him is returned the knowledge of the Hour.” The knowledge of when the Day of Judgment will occur belongs exclusively to Allah ﷻ. No one else has access to this information—it returns to Him alone.

Then Allah ﷻ describes the scope of His knowledge: وَمَا تَخْرُجُ مِن ثَمَرَٰتٍ مِّنْ أَكْمَامِهَا—“And no fruits emerge from their coverings…” The word أَكْمَامِ refers to the outer covering or sheath, like the peel of grapes or the protective covering around dates that come in bunches. Every fruit that emerges from its protective covering does so with Allah’s knowledge.

وَمَا تَحْمِلُ مِنْ أُنثَىٰ وَلَا تَضَعُ إِلَّا بِعِلْمِهِۦ—“And no female carries nor gives birth except with His knowledge.” The word أُنثَىٰ doesn’t just mean a human woman—it includes any female: a cow, a bird, any creature. Every conception and every birth of every female creature occurs within Allah’s perfect knowledge.

وَيَوْمَ يُنَادِيهِمْ أَيْنَ شُرَكَآءِى—“And on the Day when He will call to them: ‘Where are My partners?’” This is the ultimate question for the mushrikun. Where are those beings you claimed were partners with Allah ﷻ?

قَالُوٓا۟ ءَاذَنَّـٰكَ مَا مِنَّا مِن شَهِيدٍ—“They will say: ‘We announce to You—there is no witness among us.’” On that Day, they will acknowledge that no one among them can testify to the existence of any partners with Allah ﷻ. Their false claims will be exposed, and they will have no defense.

Ayah 47–54 · linked video: 06. Fussilat (Ayah 47-54)

Surah Fussilat: Ayahs 47-54

Ayah 47

۞ إِلَيْهِ يُرَدُّ عِلْمُ ٱلسَّاعَةِ ۚ وَمَا تَخْرُجُ مِن ثَمَرَٰتٍ مِّنْ أَكْمَامِهَا وَمَا تَحْمِلُ مِنْ أُنثَىٰ وَلَا تَضَعُ إِلَّا بِعِلْمِهِۦ ۚ وَيَوْمَ يُنَادِيهِمْ أَيْنَ شُرَكَآءِى قَالُوٓا۟ ءَاذَنَّـٰكَ مَا مِنَّا مِن شَهِيدٍ

The knowledge of the Hour returns to Him alone. Nothing emerges from fruits as they burst from their coverings, no female carries or delivers, except with His knowledge. This ayah establishes a fundamental principle: all knowledge of the unseen, particularly the timing of the Day of Judgment, belongs exclusively to Allah ﷻ.

The imagery Allah ﷻ uses here is remarkable in its precision. He compares mothers giving birth to fruits being peeled from their protective coverings. Just as the peel protects the fruit within, the mother’s womb protects the child. The Arabic word ثَمَرَٰت for fruits creates a linguistic connection, because in the Quran and classical Arabic, children are also referred to as the “fruits” of their parents. Nothing emerges from these protective coverings except that Allah ﷻ has complete knowledge of it—when it will emerge, how it will emerge, and what its condition will be.

The ayah then shifts to Judgment Day itself: وَيَوْمَ يُنَادِيهِمْ أَيْنَ شُرَكَآءِى - “And on the day when He will call them and say, ‘Where are those partners of Mine?’” This is a devastating question. The disbelievers who spent their entire lives calling upon false gods and associates will be confronted directly by Allah ﷻ.

Their response reveals complete surrender: قَالُوٓا۟ ءَاذَنَّـٰكَ مَا مِنَّا مِن شَهِيدٍ - “They will say, ‘We seek Your permission to speak—there is not among us a single witness.’” The phrase آذَنَّـٰكَ is remarkable. It means “we seek your permission” or “we’re ready to listen to You now.” It’s as if they’re saying, “Sorry, sorry, we’re ready to listen now.” They’ve completely abandoned their false religions. Not a single one of them will dare to stand as a witness supporting their former beliefs. They’ve all converted, but far too late.

Ayah 48

وَضَلَّ عَنْهُم مَّا كَانُوا۟ يَدْعُونَ مِن قَبْلُ ۖ وَظَنُّوا۟ مَا لَهُم مِّن مَّحِيصٍ

Everything they used to call upon has been lost from them. All the false gods, all the idols, all the intermediaries they relied upon—completely vanished. The word ضَلَّ indicates something that has strayed away, become lost, or disappeared entirely.

They come to a devastating realization: وَظَنُّوا۟ مَا لَهُم مِّن مَّحِيصٍ - “And they realize with certainty that they have no place of escape.” The word مَّحِيصٍ describes an impossible situation from which there’s no escape. It’s derived from حَاصَ, which means to flee from difficulty. A mahees is that impossible place where, when there was no chance of surviving, somehow you found shelter. You’ve seen those action scenes where an explosion happens and someone hides under a rock and somehow survives while everything else is destroyed—that’s a mahees. Allah ﷻ is saying: don’t worry, there won’t be any such place. There’s no shelter, no hiding spot, no escape route whatsoever.

Ayah 49

لَّا يَسْـَٔمُ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنُ مِن دُعَآءِ ٱلْخَيْرِ وَإِن مَّسَّهُ ٱلشَّرُّ فَيَـُٔوسٌ قَنُوطٌ

The human being never gets tired of asking for good things. The verb يَسْـَٔمُ means to become weary, exhausted, or fed up. This isn’t necessarily limited to dua to Allah ﷻ—it’s about calling out for, pursuing, and desiring good things in general.

Think about human nature: you’re exhausted after a long day. You come home completely drained and tell your family you need hours of rest. But the moment something of interest appears—something you really want—that exhaustion vanishes instantly. You jump at the opportunity. When it comes to pursuing what we desire, we never get tired. A student might stay up all night before an exam they haven’t studied for, making lengthy dua: “Ya Allah, please don’t let me fail this test.” They don’t tire of that dua.

But notice the contrast: وَإِن مَّسَّهُ ٱلشَّرُّ فَيَـُٔوسٌ قَنُوطٌ - “But when harm touches him, he becomes hopeless and despairing.” The words يَـُٔوسٌ and قَنُوطٌ both indicate extreme despair, but they compound each other. Ya’us describes someone completely cut off from hope—hope is dead for them. Qanut adds another dimension: extreme pessimism. Not only is he hopeless about what happened, he’s pessimistic that any good will ever come his way again. Everything is ruined. It’s all over.

We all know people like this—moody individuals who, when upset, completely tank. They shut down, stop talking, withdraw from everyone. No matter how much you try to cheer them up or reassure them that it’s okay, they remain in that state. This is the weakness of human nature that Allah ﷻ is exposing.

Ayah 50

وَلَئِنْ أَذَقْنَـٰهُ رَحْمَةً مِّنَّا مِنۢ بَعْدِ ضَرَّآءَ مَسَّتْهُ لَيَقُولَنَّ هَـٰذَا لِى وَمَآ أَظُنُّ ٱلسَّاعَةَ قَآئِمَةً وَلَئِن رُّجِعْتُ إِلَىٰ رَبِّىٓ إِنَّ لِى عِندَهُۥ لَلْحُسْنَىٰ ۚ فَلَنُنَبِّئَنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ بِمَا عَمِلُوا۟ وَلَنُذِيقَنَّهُم مِّنْ عَذَابٍ غَلِيظٍ

When We make him taste mercy from Us after harm has touched him, he is absolutely guaranteed to say: هَـٰذَا لِى - “This is mine. I deserved it.”

This is observable even in children. Imagine a child obsessed with toy cars. You go to the store and come home, and he asks eagerly, “Did you get my car? Did you get it?” You say no, you got something for his brother instead. There’s crying—and you need to understand there’s a difference between real crying and drama crying. There’s the cry from falling down the stairs, and then there’s the performance cry. “I never get anything!” The whole show begins.

Then you say, “Oh wait, I did get you something. You can have this one, but just for five minutes.” You try to hand it to him, but first make sure he understands: “How long can you have it for?” He’s trying to grab it: “Five minutes.” “Say it properly.” “Five minutes!” “Okay, here.”

He plays with it. Five minutes pass. “Time’s up, give it back.” What does he say? “But it’s mine! You can’t take it—it’s MINE!”

This is the attitude Allah ﷻ is describing: لَيَقُولَنَّ هَـٰذَا لِى. When We make them taste mercy from Us, they immediately say, “This is mine.” Not “Allah gave this to me.” Not even “Dad gave this to me.” It’s “I earned this. I deserve this.”

This attitude of entitlement creates a twisted worldview that mutates like a virus. It develops to the point where the person thinks: “Allah keeps giving me these things because I deserve them. Allah gives me everything I have because I’m entitled to it. And if that’s the case, this will probably never end.”

This is where the spiritual corruption deepens: وَمَآ أَظُنُّ ٱلسَّاعَةَ قَآئِمَةً - “And I don’t think the Hour is even coming.” Or if it is coming, “It’s not for me. I deserve better.”

Then it goes even further: وَلَئِن رُّجِعْتُ إِلَىٰ رَبِّىٓ إِنَّ لِى عِندَهُۥ لَلْحُسْنَىٰ - “And if I am returned to my Master, surely He will have the very best there for me.” This echoes what we learned in Surah Fussilat earlier. The person becomes completely delusional, thinking their materialism and worldly success are proof that Allah ﷻ loves them, proof that He’s on their side. “If He’s hooked me up so much in this world, I can only imagine the party He’ll throw for me in Jannah!”

This mutation in thinking, this delusional entitlement, is being described as disbelief itself. Allah ﷻ warns: فَلَنُنَبِّئَنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ بِمَا عَمِلُوا۟ - “We will thoroughly inform those who disbelieved of what they did,” وَلَنُذِيقَنَّهُم مِّنْ عَذَابٍ غَلِيظٍ - “and We will make them taste harsh punishment.”

The word غَلِيظٍ is particularly powerful here—harsh, tough, with no flexibility in it. People who get used to luxury cry out when even small things are taken away. Their tolerance for difficulty diminishes to nothing. Those who are spoiled can’t handle even minor inconvenience—“Start the car ten minutes before I get in so the AC is perfect. I can’t handle it otherwise.” For such people, Allah ﷻ says, just taking away their luxuries is punishment enough. I’ll give them ghaleeth—harsh punishment with no softness in it.

Ayah 51

وَإِذَآ أَنْعَمْنَا عَلَى ٱلْإِنسَـٰنِ أَعْرَضَ وَنَـَٔا بِجَانِبِهِۦ وَإِذَا مَسَّهُ ٱلشَّرُّ فَذُو دُعَآءٍ عَرِيضٍ

When We shower the human being with favor, he turns away. The word أَعْرَضَ indicates ignoring, showing indifference. He just forgets where his blessings came from.

وَنَـَٔا بِجَانِبِهِۦ - “And he turns arrogantly to his side.” The phrase نَـَٔا بِجَانِبِهِۦ captures someone turning away with arrogance, pivoting to pursue whatever worldly interests he wants to chase instead of showing gratitude.

But watch what happens: وَإِذَا مَسَّهُ ٱلشَّرُّ فَذُو دُعَآءٍ عَرِيضٍ - “When harm touches him, then he becomes one of extensive dua.” The word عَرِيضٍ means broad, extensive, lengthy. Suddenly that person becomes incredibly spiritual. His prayers become long and desperate: “Please, please, please! I’m sorry, I’m sorry! No, no, I really mean it this time!”

Watch someone during turbulence on a plane—even the person who was just listening to music and texting inappropriate conversations. Then the cop pulls him over. Before the officer even gets out of the car, watch what happens. The music pauses. The phone is hung up. He’s sitting there making dua. The cop takes his time walking up, letting him squirm. That’s when the dua becomes most intense.

This is the direct continuation of what we’ve been learning throughout this surah. When is your dua valid? When you’ve changed your lifestyle. When it’s not just in times of trouble that you turn to Allah ﷻ, but when He’s your priority at all other times as well. That’s the message of this surah, and now we’re seeing it in full force.

Ayah 52

قُلْ أَرَءَيْتُمْ إِن كَانَ مِنْ عِندِ ٱللَّهِ ثُمَّ كَفَرْتُم بِهِۦ مَنْ أَضَلُّ مِمَّنْ هُوَ فِى شِقَاقٍۭ بَعِيدٍ

Allah ﷻ instructs His Messenger ﷺ: قُلْ - “Say to them,” أَرَءَيْتُمْ إِن كَانَ مِنْ عِندِ ٱللَّهِ - “Have you considered—just the possibility—if this Quran is from Allah,” ثُمَّ كَفَرْتُم بِهِۦ - “and then you disbelieved in it?”

This is addressing the disbeliever directly: Fine, you don’t think Islam is the truth. You don’t think there’s any possibility the Quran is from Allah ﷻ—no possibility whatsoever. But Allah ﷻ challenges: haven’t you at least considered the hypothetical? Wouldn’t it bother you to even think about the possibility that if it is from Allah ﷻ, how much trouble you’d be in?

مَنْ أَضَلُّ مِمَّنْ هُوَ فِى شِقَاقٍۭ بَعِيدٍ - “Who could be more misguided than one who is in extreme opposition, far removed from the truth?” The word شِقَاقٍ comes from شَقَّ—to split, to break. It describes a deep schism, like a rock breaking in two with the pieces falling on opposite sides of a mountain. Who could be worse off than someone in that state of complete separation from guidance?

Ayah 53

سَنُرِيهِمْ ءَايَـٰتِنَا فِى ٱلْـَٔافَاقِ وَفِىٓ أَنفُسِهِمْ حَتَّىٰ يَتَبَيَّنَ لَهُمْ أَنَّهُ ٱلْحَقُّ ۗ أَوَلَمْ يَكْفِ بِرَبِّكَ أَنَّهُۥ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ شَهِيدٌ

سَنُرِيهِمْ ءَايَـٰتِنَا - “We will soon show them Our signs,” فِى ٱلْـَٔافَاقِ - “in the horizons,” وَفِىٓ أَنفُسِهِمْ - “and inside their own selves.”

Allah ﷻ has already shown us the miracle of the Quran. He has already placed signs in ourselves and in the horizons—look at the stars, the sun, the moon, the morning, the night, the ground, the plants, the animals, the birds, the trees. The Quran constantly tells us to observe all of this. Even the human being himself: فَلْيَنظُرِ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنُ مِمَّ خُلِقَ - “Let man consider from what he was created” (86:5).

But this ayah speaks of something special: signs that will be shown in the future. حَتَّىٰ يَتَبَيَّنَ لَهُمْ أَنَّهُ ٱلْحَقُّ - “Until it becomes absolutely clear to them that this Quran is the truth.”

This requires a balanced understanding. On one hand, human beings have looked at the sky for centuries, but are we looking deeper than ever before? Absolutely. Have people examined themselves before? Yes, but are we looking deeper inside ourselves than ever before in human history? Through medicine, biology, neurology, psychology—we know more about ourselves now than ever before. We know more about the universe than ever in history.

Allah ﷻ says that as this exploration continues, the ayat of the Quran will continue to manifest. Those who reflect on the Quran and also reflect on the universe, on the human being, on psychology, on medicine, on all fields of knowledge—they will begin to see parallels. They’ll discover that the Quran said something long ago that matches exactly what we’ve just discovered now.

This has become known as the scientific miracles of the Quran. However, there’s an important disclaimer: we shouldn’t force science onto the Quran. We shouldn’t desperately search for scientific phenomena to label as miracles by twisting Quranic meanings. But there are some things that have become so obvious, so clear, that they genuinely make you reflect.

Consider وَٱلسَّمَآءِ وَٱلطَّارِقِ (86:1) - “By the sky and the knocker” (At-Tariq). Modern listening devices detecting signals from the farthest galaxies describe knocking sounds. The word was already there—you don’t have to reinterpret it.

Or الْعَلَقَ (al-’alaq) - the clinging clot that hangs from the upper wall of the mother’s womb. They didn’t know at the time that the embryo literally clings and hangs. The word ’alaq in Arabic means “to hang,” and people like Ibn Kathir (may Allah have mercy on him) simply said it’s a piece of flesh, not understanding why it was called a “hanging” piece of flesh.

Or وَأَنزَلْنَا ٱلْحَدِيدَ (57:25) - “And We sent down iron.” Allah ﷻ says He sent iron down, meaning it’s not from the earth itself. Scientists who study geology have discovered that iron ore requires far too much energy and heat to be produced on Earth. The dominant theory is that it came via meteors that crashed into the planet. Allah ﷻ didn’t say He sent down copper, silver or gold—specifically iron.

These aren’t things to print in a handout and distribute to science students as a conversion pamphlet. Rather, these are discoveries for you to make yourself, for you to see with your own eyes. And yes, non-Muslims study these sciences far more than Muslims do right now. But Muslims should be at the forefront of these fields. Why? Because Allah ﷻ tells us to reflect on His creation. For that reason, we should study it more than anyone else. And the more we study it, the more spiritual we should become.

What’s remarkable about our times is how deluded humanity has become. There’s an interesting parallel some scholars have noted about the Dajjal (the Antichrist) being described as one-eyed. Allah ﷻ has given human beings two eyes, and He’s given us two types of vision: the physical world and the unseen world. When Allah ﷻ created Adam (AS), He gave him two kinds of knowledge—وَعَلَّمَ ءَادَمَ ٱلْأَسْمَآءَ كُلَّهَا (2:31) - worldly knowledge of physical objects, and guidance from Allah ﷻ, which is otherworldly knowledge.

We’re meant to see the world with both eyes—the eye of the physical world and the eye of the spiritual dimension. What happens when one eye closes? Usually the spiritual eye closes, leaving only the worldly perspective. You become one-eyed, materialistic. You can study the universe but won’t see anything beyond the physical universe. You can study medicine but won’t see anything beyond biological mechanics.

But a believer with both eyes open gazes into the universe and their iman soars. Muslims studying medicine and the human body become more in awe of Allah ﷻ than anything else.

Now consider the opposite—the Dajjal parallel. What does the Dajjal claim to be eventually? God. People who reach the highest levels of scientific expertise—advanced engineering, microchip technology, cutting-edge surgery—often develop what psychologists literally call the “God complex.” They believe they know more than anyone else, that everyone else is primitive compared to them. When you have only one eye open, seeing only the material world, you eventually call yourself god. That’s what the one-eyed Dajjal does.

The world is increasingly becoming one-eyed. And what does the Dajjal offer if you follow him? Beauty in this world—turning deserts green, rapid travel across the earth, all the material comforts. That’s the heart of materialism.

Allah ﷻ says: We will show them inside themselves and in the horizons, the signs, until it becomes absolutely clear to them that this is the truth. But for that clarity to come, which eye must be open? The eye of guidance must be opened. When it’s closed, the ayat will be manifest but nobody will see anything.

أَوَلَمْ يَكْفِ بِرَبِّكَ أَنَّهُۥ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ شَهِيدٌ - “Isn’t it enough for your Master that He is a witness over all things?” Before we discover anything, before we see any sign, isn’t the fact that Allah ﷻ witnesses everything sufficient? Up to Allah ﷻ is what doors of knowledge He opens for us. Who knows—before we die, Allah ﷻ might even show us what the seven heavens truly are. Human beings have discovered more now than ever before, and this is entirely up to Allah ﷻ.

Ayah 54

أَلَآ إِنَّهُمْ فِى مِرْيَةٍ مِّن لِّقَآءِ رَبِّهِمْ ۗ أَلَآ إِنَّهُۥ بِكُلِّ شَىْءٍ مُّحِيطٌۢ

أَلَآ إِنَّهُمْ فِى مِرْيَةٍ - “Know that they are in doubt,” مِّن لِّقَآءِ رَبِّهِمْ - “about meeting their Master.”

The word مِرْيَةٍ describes a doubt that shouldn’t exist—doubt about something that is certain. It comes from مَارَى, to doubt or dispute. They’re in doubt about meeting Allah ﷻ, doubting the resurrection, doubting accountability—a doubt that has no legitimate basis.

أَلَآ إِنَّهُۥ بِكُلِّ شَىْءٍ مُّحِيطٌۢ - “They had better know that Allah has completely encompassed, enveloped, surrounded all things.” Everything is within His grasp. Nothing escapes His knowledge, His power, His control. This is both a warning and a promise: no matter how much they doubt, no matter how much they deny, Allah ﷻ has them completely encompassed. There is no escape from meeting Him.

With this, the surah concludes its powerful message about human nature, accountability, and the undeniable reality of the return to Allah ﷻ.
